### JanSz — 2021-08-21T17:34:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/statins-the-real-info-you-need-to-grasp.8641/post-302678>

> [Da-mo said:](/goto/post?id=300658)
>
> I am a man under 65 (53) who had a heart attack 4 months ago resulting in a stent. I have been on Atorvastatin 40mg/day for the last 4 months.
>
> From the research I can see that , at best, if I stay on this drug for 4.5 years it may extend my lifespan, not my health-span, by 5.3 days. WTF??
>
> I really don't like how the drug works by blocking HMGCoA reductase - which many people know also affects CoQ10 and hence lowers ATP.
>
> What many people - (including my GP) - do not know is that another effect of blocking that pathway is that it also blocks Mevalonate. This is the rate limiter for PCSK9 so PCSK9 and NARC1 are upregulated. This blocks the LDL receptors on the hepatocyte, thus defeating the increased expression of the LDL receptors associated with the statin.
> [https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/01.atv.0000134621.14315.43](https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/01.atv.0000134621.14315.43)
>
> So why am I still taking it? There are other pleiotropic effects of the drug - some beneficial, some not. I'm doing my own research in order to make an informed choice rather than panic either way.#skininthegame
>
> On the subject of trading one set of risks for another, here's something about how lipophyllic versus hydrophyllic statins double dementia risk.
> [https://jnm.snmjournals.org/content/62/supplement\_1/102](https://jnm.snmjournals.org/content/62/supplement_1/102)

### Da-mo — 2021-12-22T03:42:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/statins-the-real-info-you-need-to-grasp.8641/post-307813>

So - back in August my lipid panel, particularly LDL, was the worst it has ever been despite 6 months of Atorvastatin 40mg daily.

I subsequently reduced the statin to 10mg and implemented intermittent fasting one meal a day for 4 straight days on every 8 day cycle.

2 weeks ago I went in for echocardiogram and cardiologist consultation. The cardiologist told me I had made a miraculous recovery. My ejection fraction was now >55 (previously 45) and the full movement had returned to the affected heart muscle. You would think she would have asked me what I did to recover, right? She didn't. She wasn't very happy about me reducing the statin either.

I put this recovery down to going fairly keto and intermittent fasting. IF is such a powerful tool and Autophagy Rocks!!!

### Jack Kruse — 2022-09-16T02:18:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/statins-the-real-info-you-need-to-grasp.8641/post-315747>

Statins are mitochondrial toxins because of their effect on CoEnzQ10. There are many ways in which they increase coronary artery calcification. One way is depletion of Vitamin K2 from the gut, another is lack of sun to control calcium flows, and another is direct arterial melanopsin damage liberating Vitamin A to cause intimal damage and a loss of arterial NO.

Sufficient production of vital biochemicals such as Geranylgeraniol (GGPP) is required to maintain endotoxin tolerance in macrophages in our arteries once the damage occurs. Macrophages are the hallmarks of CVD/Atherosclerosis, contributing to plaque development, inflammation, and the promotion of thrombosis. Geranylgeraniol is downstream of Mevalonate in the cholesterol synthesis pathway, and GGPP synthesis is inhibited by Statins, as is CoQ10 and K2. Vitamin K2 is the cofactor for matrix Gla-protein activation, which PROTECTS arteries from calcification.

Statin use is independently associated with increased calcification in patients, & using an animal model of hypercholesterolemia, we present a molecular mechanism whereby statins promote the calcification of atherosclerotic plaque. [https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/ATVBAHA.119.313832](https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/ATVBAHA.119.313832)

Statins, Muscles

### [What statins do to muscles](https://www.mdc-berlin.de/news/press/what-statins-do-muscles)

Muscle pain and cramps are common side effects of statins. As an MDC and Charité research group now reports in Scientific Reports, the cholesterol reducers actually influence thousands of genes in muscle cells – thereby hindering cell growth and division.

 www.mdc-berlin.de

### Jack Kruse — 2024-04-17T19:40:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/statins-the-real-info-you-need-to-grasp.8641/post-333726>

> [Da-mo said:](/goto/post?id=300688)
>
> Bringing this back on topic, I'll attempt to show below how and why a statin can be ineffective in lowering LDL when on a low carbohydrate diet . . . .
>
> Here is my lipid panel taken in Feb 2021 immediately post myocardial infarction.
> Total 5.3 mmol/L
> Triglyceride 1.2 mmol/L
> HDL Cholesterol 1.18 mmol/L
> LDL Cholesterol - calculated 3.6 mmol/L
>
> Here is my lipid panel 4 months later in June 2021 after 4 months on Atorvastatin at 40 mg/day
> Total 5.8 mmol/L
> Triglyceride 1.6 mmol/L
> HDL Cholesterol 0.98 mmol/L
> LDL Cholesterol - calculated 4.1 mmol/L
>
> Note that every aspect got worse!!!
>
> In the few days before the June blood test I switched to LCHF. The Feldman protocol shows how this can cause a high LDL result.
>
>
> [@JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) A lot to go through in your post. Thank you.
>
> Reducing/reversing arterial plaque can be done by elevating HDL (reverse cholesterol transport) and by up-regulating autophagy (recycling of damaged/broken/unnecessary tissue). I'm using intermittent fasting to up-regulate autophagy.
>
>
> Rapamycin can also up-regulate autophagy.
>
>
> [https://www.nature.com/articles/cdd2008110](https://www.nature.com/articles/cdd2008110)
>
> However, I would rather use an endogenous process to accomplish that than an exogenous one because there is a risk of making the plaques unstable if it happens too rapidly. Apparently plaque stabilization is one of the pleiotropic effects of statins. Another pleiotropic effect is as an anti-inflammatory which still happens at very low doses.

Now Nick Horowitz PhD med student has showed how eating Oreo's is better than a statin for lowering your cholesterol. Let that sink in. It tells you how FOS they have been.

Audrey I think you need to be careful with the sun where you live. Stay in the shade during the hours when the sun is at its strongest. You can rest in the shade of a big tree or something
Even here (!) so far north, I can feel it some days in summer the sun is too strong to be in for too long. Not often but I do feel it. Especially if I have been outside for days in a row getting much sun... it is like my body says.. enough! And I listen to it. Always listen to your body, it will tell you! Maybe you have become more sensitive to you needs / the needs of your body. This is a very good thing

I don't do well with SPF creams on my face (I've tried a few!) so I just wear a hat with a nice wide brim if I'm outside between 11am and 3.30pm or so - though I might even need to extend those hours. My kids have to wear a wide brimmed hat even to be allowed out to play at school in Terms 4 and 1 (That's mid October - early April)...guess it's similar in Aus for school kids?

My colouring has changed too since taking up CT, and my sun tolerance has increased a lot i.e. I used to find it completely draining and now I find it energising (outside the "danger hours")!
....But even last Summer I couldn't be in the midday sun. After a few minutes I'd feel that warning prickling you get before starting to burn.
You might have better luck than me finding a cream for your face.

### caroline — 2013-11-03T07:56:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-6-the-ancient-pathway.8644/post-99703>

I reread C/T #6 today. This is a tough one to get thru for so many reasons ......it is just so important for us to get it ... and just do it!

Two of Dr. K.'s final statements ... "I suggest you tattoo it to the inside of your eyelids and remember it when you need it the most"

"if you want to live and live well, become cold adapted A.S.A.P."

I have been struggling with this lately. I take cold showers all the time and swim in the ocean .... but I really should be sitting in a tub of ice more - I have turned into a wimp for some reason.

I do have all kinds of really good excuses ..... but that doesn't get the job done....

It is so easy to become complacent ... and lazy....

I gave myself every advantage today when I was reading the blog .... I sat on the grass, under a huge, majestic conifer and right next to the ocean......just lovely........however - I think I am becoming way too intense ...... kinda scary to people - me thinks ....

Once Dr.K. mentioned non native EMF .....they fled to the hills......

I actually have more luck with friends and loved ones...... they intuitively are sceptical of EMF...and they haven't been reading the blogs for 3 years..... and become cross eyed from reading all this science stuff.....

### QuantumRadical — 2013-11-04T00:10:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-6-the-ancient-pathway.8644/post-99746>

It will be a generation or two before "they all" get it. Even after the earth was discovered to be a sphere and not flat, I am sure the schools back then took a couple of generations to switch from "the earth is flat" to "the earth is round". Too much BIG MONEY is tied up in the status quo. Even smart people will deny reality if it doesn't match their beliefs. And we have been significantly brainwashed by media, marketing, advertising, etc. to believe what we believe about food, medicine, and health. Let's face it, to be healthy just do the opposite of what everybody believes is the truth. Fat makes you fat--so eat more fat and get skinny. Exercise burns calories so you lose weight--exercise just makes you hungrier so you eat more and gain more weight. Cold will make you sick--cold improves your immune system so embrace the cold tub. I could go on and on about what many believe is common sense, but is not. Being contrarian may not make you more friends, but may save your life.

I have been reading this forum and Jack's Blogs for a good 2.5 years. I got it immediately because my nature has always been to challenge/question authority and I have a scientific/technical background. But I also checked out every cite from the Blog's to make sure that Jack's interpretation was on the money. So far, 100%! Most people go with the flow, and would never even think to challenge the expertise of a doctor, or an institution, or even a government. But, that is not how we make progress. Those who challenge are the ones that make progress. But also remember, the explorers are trampled by the pioneers who are subsequently shot in the back by an arrow by the settlers. And so the world goes around.

Ultimately, the laws of physics prevail. We are just starting to realize the implications of what that means from a biology, health, and medicine perspective. Ultimately, those implications will ripple through everything in our lives just as a round earth transformed the world back in the day.

I ramble.

Q'Rad Randy

### caroline — 2013-11-04T00:23:32+0200

### caroline — 2013-11-04T00:34:43+0200

The thing is ..... They always say to me - but people are living much longer these days ....and I don't have a good enough response.

I was up close and personal with dementia \[myDH\] that is not something you ever, ever want a loved one to go thru. It is heartbreaking and tears you apart - for ever. To see a strong person slowly lose everything they know and hold dear is not something you ever want to see .......but the odds are stacked against us if we don't change the state of our existence. We really need to fight for our life and the life of our loved ones...if only they would let us.

Someday it will become crystal clear to them - but will it be in time......

### nicld — 2013-11-04T01:49:17+0200

We may be living longer but definitely the quality of life is just not there. Like your DH, my dear FIL died of Alzheimer's and it just broke my heart. It took me almost 6 months after he went in the nursing home to see him since my grandfather also died from it. They were both once strong proud farmer who were bed ridden and did not know who anyone was. It is one of the worse diseases EVER. Then when my MIL died on heart failure the SIL took out a huge box pill from her house that kept her alive at one point. And then the SIL is not doing much better. She is just in her early 60's, has had a stroke already and is diabetic and basically disabled. She is great motivation for me to do everything I can to get optimal. ### Inger — 2013-11-04T08:05:10+0200

Hey Gang, I want to get your opinion on my routine. I have been doing CT for 28 minutes at 43-45f around 3pm and then riding my bike up to this park to do a series of exercises in the MovNat style - sprints, push-ups, pull-ups from a tree branch, dips, deep body squats, and then I get creative carrying logs over head doing split squats and all sort of other movements. Then I ride back and wait an hour then do CT again the same way. I like to wait an hour before doing the second CT session based on what I read from Dr. Rhonda Patrick about allowing the muscle autophagy response to signal before hitting the cold. I feel like get more power and more reps when I do the CT before my workout circuits. What do you guys think of this routine? It feels really good!

### PaulG — 2013-11-04T05:15:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-to-start-on-educating-people-on-the-dangers-of-non-native-emfs.8649/post-99774>

Someone I know has asked the question can you get sick from electricity / EMFs?

To be honest I am at a loss as to know where to start?

1) Verbal arguments. Its hard to convince someone with words without coming across as a mad person (which is generally how I am perceived in my family as they cant be bothered to research).

2) Link to websites - trouble with this is that you can find plenty of people saying there is no evidence etc. Some websites such as Jack Kruse might be too advanced for a teenager or someone first considering the concept.

3) Appeal to authority - what organisations are coming out against mobile phones/towers, WiFi? The World Health Organisation

I sure people can relate to this situation so would be interested in approaches and resources to help bring enlightenment.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-04T05:48:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-to-start-on-educating-people-on-the-dangers-of-non-native-emfs.8649/post-99775>

Very Simple........have them read Andrew Marino's treatise on his and Becker's work and all the testimony they gave in the book "Going Somewhere." I have yet to see one person who has read it come away not getting it.

**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### enyaw — 2013-11-04T20:46:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-ast-alt.8653/post-99823>

These are my wife's labs. She's worried about the high ALT/AST. I'm concerned with her BUN:Creatinine ratio; it's approaching 20. Dehydration?
We have a 9 month old. She's 29 yo 5'7" 125lbs. Anybody have any insight into the ALT/AST scores?

Component Your Value Standard Range Units
Calcium 9.55 8.00 - 10.50 mg/dL
Alkaline Phosphatase 85 0 - 120 U/L
Bilirubin, Total 0.4 0.2 - 1.2 mg/dL
Protein, Total 8.2 6.0 - 8.0 g/dL
Albumin 4.6 3.6 - 5.0 g/dL
**BUN 12 6 - 23 mg/dL
Creatinine, Serum 0.63 0.60 - 1.40 mg/dL**
Glucose, Random 82 65 - 99 mg/dL
**AST 114 10 - 40 U/L
ALT 212 2 - 45 U/L**
Sodium 140 135 - 145 mEq/L
Potassium 4.1 3.5 - 4.9 mEq/L
Chloride 105 96 - 110 mEq/L
CO2, Total 28.0 20.0 - 30.0 mmol/L
Anion Gap 7 2 - 16
TSH - Ultrasensitive 1.84 0.35 - 4.01 uIU/mL
Vitamin B12 877 211 - 911 pg/mL
**Folate >24.00 >5.38 ng/mL**
WBC 6.1 4.0 - 12.0 K/uL
RBC 4.43 3.50 - 5.55 M/uL
Hemoglobin 13.4 12.0 - 15.0 g/dL
Hematocrit 39.3 36.0 - 45.0 %
MCV 89 80 - 100 fL
MCH 30.2 27.0 - 33.0 pg
MCHC 34.1 31.0 - 37.0 g/dL
Platelet Count 232 150 - 400 K/uL

### Bonniebelle — 2013-11-04T21:33:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-ast-alt.8653/post-99829>

I'm sure someone else can probably answer this question better, but I will give you my feedback.

**About elevated liver enzymes:**

I know they are part of the liver function test. I remember having elevated levels at one time when I was working with a personal trainer and had really increased my protein intake. I had been to the gym right before having my labs drawn. My doctor seemed to think it wasn't anything to be alarmed about and the next time we checked my levels were normal. Other than that I don't remember specifically what each means so I had to google it. Looks like AST is associated with muscles as well as the liver so if your wife really increased exercise or protein intake maybe that is the reason. But I would certainly check with your wife's doctor to rule out any other issues. My mother's liver enzymes were very elevated and that is what led to the discovery that she had pancreatic cancer. The elevated levels led to a sonogram, then CT scan and they found the pancreatic mass.

What do high (elevated) liver tests (AST and ALT) mean?

AST (SGOT) and ALT (SGPT) are sensitive indicators of liver damage or injury from different types of diseases or conditions. But it must be emphasized that higher-than-normal levels of these liver enzymes should not be automatically equated with liver disease. They may mean liver problems or they may not. For example, elevations of these enzymes can occur with muscle damage. The interpretation of elevated AST and ALT results depends upon the entire clinical evaluation of an individual, and so it is best done by physicians experienced in evaluating liver disease and muscle disease.

Moreover, the precise levels of these liver enzyme test do not correlate well with the extent of liver problems or the prognosis (outlook). Thus, the exact levels of AST (SGOT) and ALT (SGPT) cannot be used to determine the degree of liver disease or predict the future.

[http://www.medicinenet.com/liver\_blood\_tests/page3.htm](http://www.medicinenet.com/liver_blood_tests/page3.htm)

Elevated liver enzymes may indicate inflammation or damage to cells in the liver. Inflamed or injured liver cells leak higher than normal amounts of certain chemicals, including liver enzymes, into the bloodstream, which can result in elevated liver enzymes on blood tests.

The specific elevated liver enzymes most commonly found are:

Alanine transaminase (ALT)
Aspartate transaminase (AST)
Elevated liver enzymes may be discovered during routine blood testing. In most cases, liver enzyme levels are only mildly and temporarily elevated. Most of the time, elevated liver enzymes don't signal a chronic, serious liver problem.

[http://www.mayoclinic.com/health/elevated-liver-enzymes/my00508](http://www.mayoclinic.com/health/elevated-liver-enzymes/my00508)

**About BUN/Creatinine ration:**

Yes, I remember Dr. Kruse saying that a high BUN/Creatinine ratio meant dehydration.

I found this calculator for BUN/Creatinine ration because some of my labs did not have the ratio listed.

[http://www.miniwebtool.com/bun-to-creatinine-ratio-calculator/](http://www.miniwebtool.com/bun-to-creatinine-ratio-calculator/)

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-11-04T21:40:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-ast-alt.8653/post-99831>

High AST and ALT indicates a liver issue. Milk thistle may help resolve this issue.

Folate is way high, and B12 is at top of the range. This is counterintuitive but probably indicates an issue with metabolizing B vitamins. At some point the 23andme profile would help anwer this question.

### Shijin13 — 2013-11-05T00:21:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-ast-alt.8653/post-99856>

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-11-05T02:03:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paying-it-forward.8655/post-99863>

Today I learned of how paying it forward can have great benefits .... if a person is ready. This story should give hope to members that get frustrated by family and friends that just don't "get it".

Awhile back I had a conversation with a relatively young guy who was scheduled to have hip replacement surgery. He said that years of playing sports had resulted in a bone on bone issue. I barely knew this guy, but had been introduced to him by one of his employees.

I shared a wide variety of JK ideas with him .... inflammation, diet, CT, EMF, etc. He seemed interested. As the conversation was ending, I encouraged him to "question everything" and get a 2nd opinion. I emailed him the JK website and included a link to Pre-Op blog.

Today I saw him, and he said he was very glad that we had the conversation. He got a 2nd opinion, and was told of a protocol, if followed to the letter, would allow him to postpone the hip replacement surgery for 5-6 years. Needless to say that made him happy.

Then, I challenged him again. I mentioned that hip replacements are typically transitional metals, and that he would be become an EMF antennae. I said why not try to make the 5 years into 10 years or more (or never).

Bottom line ..... we never know who is ready for a new viewpoint .... until we share it. This success story truly made my day!!

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-05T17:36:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/proof-of-biologic-coherence-and-macroscopic-human-qed-in-tissues.8668/post-99945>

The apparent regularity of the heartbeat conceals abundant variability, and contrary to common intuition, the more healthy the heart, the more variable the beat.
In 1993, Eugene Stanley and Ary Goldberger, and their collaborators at Boston University and Harvard Medical School in the United States published a paper showing that while the heartbeat records from healthy people look superficially very variable, there is a longtime correlation in the intervals between beats in the healthy heart. For example, shorter intervals tend to be followed by longer ones, and the effect occurs on a wide range of time scales, from seconds to many hours. In contrast, heartbeat records from heart disease patients look superficially much more regular, but are significantly less correlated when analysed mathematically.

In other words, there was less dynamic order in the heart rhythm of those afflicted with heart disease. Since then, there have been numerous studies aimed at extracting the mathematical structures that characterise the rhythm of the healthy heartbeat, in order to clearly distinguish it from those of people with heart disease or at risk from heart disease.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-08T03:34:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/proof-of-biologic-coherence-and-macroscopic-human-qed-in-tissues.8668/post-100161>

Fish oils prevent AF recurrence following PVI (pulmonary vein isolation)
You might not know that atrial fibrillation is a common sequel to coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG). In 2005 a team of Italian researchers reported that supplementation with 2 grams/day of fish oil (EPA + DHA) for 5 days prior to CABG reduced the incidence of postoperative AF from 33.3% (control group) to 15.2% (fish oil group). Now a team of researchers from St. David’s Medical Center in Austin, TX, Northeastern College of Medicine in Canton, OH, IKEM in the Czech Republic, and McMaster University in Hamilton, Canada reports that fish oil supplementation before and after a pulmonary vein isolation (PVI) procedure reduces the risk of AF recurrence.

Their retrospective study included 1500 afibbers who underwent a pulmonary vein antrum isolation (PVAI) procedure at St. David’s between 2004 and 2007. Two hundred and eighty-five patients (19%) had supplemented with a minimum of 655 mg of fish oil (in capsules) for at least a month prior to their PVAI and continued to do so during the entire follow-up period. It was possible to closely match 129 of these patients (70% paroxysmal and 30% persistent or permanent) with 129 patients (control group) from the remaining 1215 patients who had not taken fish oil. The two groups were matched for type of afib, age, gender, left atrial size, ejection fraction and the presence of type 2 diabetes, hypertension, coronary artery disease and stroke. The two groups, fish oil group and control group, were scheduled for follow-up (including 48-hr Holter monitoring) 3, 6, 9 and 12 months after the procedure and were equipped with a cardiac event monitor during the first 5 months following their PVAI.

During the blanking period (8 weeks following the procedure) 27% of patients in the fish oil group and 44% in the control group experienced afib recurrence; in other words, fish oil supplementation reduced the risk of early recurrence by 39%. Procedural failure (afib recurring more than 8 weeks from the PVAI) was 23% in the fish oil group and 32% in the control group, or a risk reduction of 28%. The research team also noted that the fish oil group had a significantly lower pre-procedure level of C-reactive protein (CRP) than did the control group (2.1 mg/L vs. 3.0 mg/L). CRP, an important marker of inflammation, measured 48 hours after the procedure was also lower in the fish oil group (14.3 mg/L vs. 18.0 mg/L). Complication rates were similar in the two study groups.

The research team concludes that patients treated with long-chain omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (fish oils) prior to and following a PVAI experienced a lower incidence of early recurrence and procedural failure compared to untreated patients.
Shaheen, PD, et al. Omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid supplementation reduced atrial fibrillation recurrence after pulmonary vein antrum isolation. Indian Pacing and Electrophysiology Journal, Vol. 9, No. 6, November 1, 2009, pp. 292-98

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-08T03:49:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/proof-of-biologic-coherence-and-macroscopic-human-qed-in-tissues.8668/post-100163>

Progression of paroxysmal AF ( a story of collagen)
Paroxysmal (intermittent, self-terminating) atrial fibrillation may over time progress to persistent or permanent afib (episodes lasting 7 days or longer). It is not clear why some paroxysmal afibbers progress to the persistent variety, while other remain paroxysmal for decades. A group of American and Italian researchers now provide at least a partial answer to this question.

Their study involved 330 patients with a history of paroxysmal AF (mean age of 70 years, 61% male) who had had a pacemaker implanted to deal with bradycardia (slow heart beat). Most study participants had underlying heart disease, but 21% were lone afibbers. The pacemaker (Medtronic AT501) automatically recorded the daily burden (duration) of afib and tachycardia for an average of 400 days. After a mean interval of 147 days, 24% of the patients progressed to persistent afib. The researchers made the following interesting observations.

The prevalence of lone atrial fibrillation (LAF) did not differ between the group that remained in paroxysmal afib and the one that progressed to persistent afib.
Patients with congestive heart failure were significantly more likely to progress to persistent AF.
Patients destined to progress to persistent AF experienced a higher daily afib burden and a higher probability of experiencing afib on any given day than those in the paroxysmal group.
The mean daily afib burden in the group destined for progression to persistent AF increased by about 14 seconds/day, while it stayed relatively constant in the group that remained paroxysmal.
Lone afibbers experienced significantly more PACs (premature atrial beats, ectopics) than did patients with CVD. However, the incidence of these ectopics decreased over time.
The conversion to persistent afib occurred suddenly and was often preceded by a period of normal sinus rhythm.
It is possible that treatment with ACE inhibitors or angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs) may slow down the remodeling that underlies progression to persistent AF.
The researchers conclude that, “Our results suggest that functional electrical remodeling may not impact all patients or inevitably lead to increasing AT/AF burden and persistent AF. In fact, a large proportion of patients may not increase their AT/AF burden, particularly in the absence of CVD.”
Saksena, S, et al. Progression of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation to persistent atrial fibrillation in patients with bradyarrhythmias. American Heart Journal, Vol. 154, November 2007, pp. 884-92

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-08T03:59:29+0200

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-08T04:09:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/proof-of-biologic-coherence-and-macroscopic-human-qed-in-tissues.8668/post-100166>

Collagen takes many forms in our body. The conduction system in the heart is one special area. All collagens share a general repeat sequence of a tri peptide of glycine, hydroxyproline, and glycine. It forms a triple helix. The smallest protein, glycine is more compact from a quantum molecular stand point so it forms the backbone where the other amino acids are wound around it. ### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-08T04:45:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/proof-of-biologic-coherence-and-macroscopic-human-qed-in-tissues.8668/post-100169>

Ok........so you got a big lesson on collagen.........what happens when collagen loses energy or increases its entropy? It forms a scar.......we call that fibrosis.

Role of fibrosis in atrial fibrillation
According to the late Professor Philippe Coumel, three conditions must be met in order for atrial fibrillation to occur:

The myocardium (heart tissue) must be capable of being triggered into and sustaining an episode. In other words, it must provide an electrophysiological substrate that is suitable for AF initiation and maintenance.
1\. The autonomic nervous system must be out of balance. (parasympathetic vs sympathetic)
2\. A trigger or precipitating event capable of initiating an episode must be present.

Researchers at the Montreal Heart Institute believe that cardiac fibrosis (formation of _scar tissue_ in response to injury) is an important feature in the development of an “a-fib friendly” substrate. Tissue fibrosis results from an accumulation of fibrillar collagen deposits which themselves are formed in a repair process aimed at replacing degenerating myocardial tissue. Fibrosis is associated with aging, dilated cardiomyopathy, mitral valve disease, and possibly myocardial ischemia (angina).

However, fibrosis and increased collagen deposition have also been observed in lone a-fibbers. Fibrosis interferes with the normal progression of the sine wave from the sinoatrial node to the atrioventricular node by impairing the transfer of the impulse from myocyte (heart muscle cell) to myocyte. The researchers point out that the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) is _involved in the formation of myocardial fibrosis_ and that patients with _primary hyperaldosteronism_ (Conn’s syndrome) have a significantly increased incidence of atrial fibrillation. They also point out that locally produced angiotensin II is associated with the formation of collagen deposits and fibrosis. Angiotensis II decreases the side chains on collagen ability to bond water by diffraction and MRI studies.

Mechanical stretch of cardiac muscle fibers induces collagen synthesis and increased angiotensin II production, thus creating structural remodeling that further promotes a-fib ( it means AF begets AF).
The researchers reason that, if the production of collagen deposits and fibrosis could be slowed or even reversed, then it may be possible to eliminate or at least control AF. They suggest that ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril, ramipril), angiotensin II type 1 receptor blockers (valsartan, irbesartan, losartan), and aldosterone antagonists (spironolactone, eplerenone, canrenone) may be useful in preventing fibrosis and thus denying the atria the electrophysiological substrate necessary for initiating and sustaining an AF episode.
Burstein, B and Nattel, S. Atrial fibrosis: mechanisms and clinical relevance in atrial fibrillation. Journal of the American College of Cardiology, Vol. 51, No. 8, February 26, 2008, pp. 802-09

### Jude — 2013-11-09T04:02:53+0200

The magnetic component of the heart’s field is about 5000 times stronger than the magnetic field produced by the brain, and can be measured 22 feet away from the body with sensitive magnetometers. ### Jack Kruse — 2025-10-28T04:24:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/proof-of-biologic-coherence-and-macroscopic-human-qed-in-tissues.8668/post-361735>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=100164)
>
> Progression of paroxysmal AF ( a story of collagen)
> Paroxysmal (intermittent, self-terminating) atrial fibrillation may over time progress to persistent or permanent afib (episodes lasting 7 days or longer). It is not clear why some paroxysmal afibbers progress to the persistent variety, while other remain paroxysmal for decades. A group of American and Italian researchers now provide at least a partial answer to this question.
>
> Their study involved 330 patients with a history of paroxysmal AF (mean age of 70 years, 61% male) who had had a pacemaker implanted to deal with bradycardia (slow heart beat). Most study participants had underlying heart disease, but 21% were lone afibbers. The pacemaker (Medtronic AT501) automatically recorded the daily burden (duration) of afib and tachycardia for an average of 400 days. After a mean interval of 147 days, 24% of the patients progressed to persistent afib. The researchers made the following interesting observations.
>
> The prevalence of lone atrial fibrillation (LAF) did not differ between the group that remained in paroxysmal afib and the one that progressed to persistent afib.
> Patients with congestive heart failure were significantly more likely to progress to persistent AF.
> Patients destined to progress to persistent AF experienced a higher daily afib burden and a higher probability of experiencing afib on any given day than those in the paroxysmal group.
> The mean daily afib burden in the group destined for progression to persistent AF increased by about 14 seconds/day, while it stayed relatively constant in the group that remained paroxysmal.
> Lone afibbers experienced significantly more PACs (premature atrial beats, ectopics) than did patients with CVD. However, the incidence of these ectopics decreased over time.
> The conversion to persistent afib occurred suddenly and was often preceded by a period of normal sinus rhythm.
> It is possible that treatment with ACE inhibitors or angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs) may slow down the remodeling that underlies progression to persistent AF.
> The researchers conclude that, â€œOur results suggest that functional electrical remodeling may not impact all patients or inevitably lead to increasing AT/AF burden and persistent AF. In fact, a large proportion of patients may not increase their AT/AF burden, particularly in the absence of CVD.â€￾
> Saksena, S, et al. Progression of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation to persistent atrial fibrillation in patients with bradyarrhythmias. American Heart Journal, Vol. 154, November 2007, pp. 884-92

Light at night and heart disease links are strong! This was just published this today in JAMA Network Open.

Using 13 million hours of personal light exposure data in >88,000 people, they found that those with the brightest nights had significantly higher risks of developing coronary artery disease, myocardial infarction, heart failure, **atrial fibrillation,** and stroke.

These associations were robust after adjusting for established cardiovascular risk factors, including physical activity, smoking, alcohol, diet, sleep duration, socioeconomic status, and polygenic risk. Keep your homes dark at night and get in the sun for photorepair of your arteries and heart!

[https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2840489](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2840489)

We've seen a lot of chatter about the webinar we had with Dr. Tim Jackson and the upcoming "Heal Your Hormones" Bootcamp. We know many of you missed part of the webinar or the whole thing so we wanted to make sure you could listen to it in it's entirety before the bootcamp starts next week.

You can listen/download to the free webinar here: [Community Wide "Heal Your Hormone" Webinar with Dr. Tim Jackson](http://www.jackkruse.com/watch/community-wide-hormones-webinar-with-dr-tim-jacksonkdkocqqozdeov9382ie/)

We hope you'll take a listen and considering joining in on the bootcamp!

In many ways, one of your best to date. Evan did a good job of asking some Q's and then letting you speak. About halfway, there was a 30 minute stretch where you were on a _roll!_

Your podcasts and interviews are some of the best communication tools in your belt. We read the science and absorb as much as we can, but when we hear it spoken with passion, intention, and clarification on the main points, we can mentally refer back to what we've read, and, like a CD player, we're able to "sample" the complex science and get the overall gist. If we want more clarity, the references you give, like which blog post, article, book, etc., help fill in the gaps that are tough to do in an hour podcast.

Thanks for taking your time to do these, Doc. Keep 'em comin'!

Martin, BGH

### caroline — 2013-11-08T23:08:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-podcast-up-with-evan-brand.8694/post-100204>

Well said Martin .....I have been saying the same thing for 2 years. Dr. K. Communicates so well in a podcast. Not near the brain pain ....and then we can go back to reading the blogs and things start to come together better....

### prAna303 — 2013-11-09T16:55:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-podcast-up-with-evan-brand.8694/post-100268>

Just to chime in, in the last three years i have gone more analog for each day. I felt that the binary world is not mine and even if i will be alone in the wild, the digital world is not for me...

Things i have done:
\- Killed my phone, mobile and mobile devices. I have i GSM, turned off and used only in emergency.
\- Killed Wifi and gears with antennas. No smart connect any more.
\- Dropped usage of computers, not listening to music that way, not seeing movies that way...
\- Interact with the ones that i resonate with, found them always in surfing, climbing, and other sports of elements but now also in the performing arts. I found that i can blend with top dancers and be like them without being a dancer, once i started there is NO WAY BACK to digital again.

So bottom line: Go Analog, ditch the digital...

# Thread: Its the little things...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-the-little-things.8700/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cantweight — 2013-11-08T17:01:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-the-little-things.8700/post-100193>

Ive been grumbling about feeling crummy and I am not alone. I have read some wonderful posts this morning that have really reconfirmed the humanity in all that we are trying to achieve. Despite setbacks we rally in a most impressive way.

4 years ago I had sick children. My then 9yr old daughter had terrible migraines, zero energy, strep in the gut, random fevers, hypothyroid, pathogens and parasites to boot. She had a tonsil and adenoidectomy, (her tonsils grew back), missed school constantly and was on a ridiculous amount of medication. My then 7yr old son was diagnosed MCTD, JRA, he was in pain constantly, fatigued, underweight, hypothyroid, high strep, pathogens, parasites, hormone imbalances. He also had the tonsils and adenoids out same day as his sister (that was fun...ugh!) also missed school constantly and was on a ton of meds.

These 2 awesome kids that could barely get through their days not that long ago are running their first 5k this weekend....a Turkey Trot! I am so proud of them!

This is after successful softball and soccer seasons. My daughter just tried out for a tournament team and my son scored his first goal last weekend.

I know these are things that other families experience on a regular basis and are probably no big deal, but I am grateful for our journey and that all these "little" things get to feel so special to us.

(My youngest is not running the race but definitely worth noting he scored the most goals on his soccer team this season....this kid that could have been stopped in his tracks by neuro tics is kicking ass!)

(Also...no one is on any prescriptions and they are doing great!!!)

### Shijin13 — 2013-11-08T18:17:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-the-little-things.8700/post-100198>

great successes Christi!!! just awesome....

### nonchalant — 2013-11-08T18:28:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-the-little-things.8700/post-100199>

It's so wonderful to hear of small children being able to live life fully again.

### caroline — 2013-11-09T00:03:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-the-little-things.8700/post-100206>

I just love to hear about all the improvements in your children Christi ..... Next up - YOU....

We take it for granted sometimes that our kids should be healthy and happy...... It is so unjust and unfair when they arent.

You have an awesome family unit ..... And you are the captain of this magnificent ship.....you are a force to be reckoned with. I would bet on you every, single time....

### Hope — 2013-11-09T01:59:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-the-little-things.8700/post-100220>

I agree with everyone. And I would add to Audrey's words....you are the captain of a magnetico and magnificent ship!!!

BTW, I would love to hear how you detoxed the kids from their biotoxins....ugh.

CONGRATS!!!!!!!!

### caroline — 2013-11-09T02:08:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-the-little-things.8700/post-100223>

Christi - have you talked to the folks at magnetico about why you are having such a struggle on your son's magnetico?
I would guess that they would have some imput - especially since you want to get one for you...

### sooperb — 2013-11-09T16:19:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-the-little-things.8700/post-100263>

I really applaud your commitment and your bravery to fight against "the system" for your children and yourself. Being a square peg in a round hold and sticking to your principles is a tough stance but you are winning, very well done Christi. I wish I had found the backbone years back (I did ban MacDonald's eventually lol) and started my children on the road to optimal, now I can only heckle them from the sidelines and I'm not convincing at least one of them that needs to listen. I won't stop though, it's too important.

### cantweight — 2013-11-12T16:25:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-the-little-things.8700/post-100504>

Thanks for the kind words! They did great and their Dad ran with them, it was awesome

Audrey...I think my problem with the magnetico is that I am loaded with metal and I was laying on it during the day which Jack said is not useful because we need to be in a chemically reduced (sleep)state. I am curious about upcoming blogs that hit on that. If we cant get to that state it seems to be a major issue although I feel I am getting there more and more. Weight still coming off slowly, sleep is decent, feeling much better than i was.

Hope....we detoxed with a ton of stuff!!! We know how different we all are and I was trying to find N=1 x 5...lol! It was nutty, but everyone is doing so well. Chlorella was the first and most constantly used, spirulina, and seaweed. I fed the kids nutritional smoothies with Opticleanse GHI or Brain Sustain has rice flour in it I believe so we havent used either in quite some time. L-glutamine, Swedish bitters, aloe. Green juices and smoothies constantly lots of cilantro and parsley and lemon. I personally used Welchol as a binder for some time. DesBio homeopathic drops Detox 1,2,3 and Metox. Lots of lemon. Trampoline jumping to help move lymph fluid. Nux vomica, berberis, NDF....so many homeopathic drops it was a bit nuts. I just wanted to leave no stone unturned. Coconut oil for antiviral properties. FNG drops for yeast, candida plus drops. Tons of probiotic...threelac, fivelac, theralac, dr ohiras, now raw milk. DE capsules, parasite cleanses, coffee enemas, supplements with bentonite clay and pectin....one was called Toxi HMF. Thats all I can think of right now.

I liken the process to more of an exorcism than a healing...lol. My poor kids....the crap I made them drink! They will all be in therapy one day...lol. But they will be healthy!!!

### SeaHorse — 2013-11-13T03:12:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-the-little-things.8700/post-100585>

Such a great success for you and your family.....thanks for sharing with everyone...I think it makes all of us feel wonderful to read these stories!

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-13T04:05:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-the-little-things.8700/post-100590>

There are many things that seem impossible only so long as one does not attempt them. To overcome this, you need to let the little things that would ordinarily bore you suddenly thrill you. The small things in life are often so much bigger than the great things . . . the trivial pleasure like cooking, one's spouse, little stories, especially sad ones, solitary walks, funny things seen and overheard. Sometimes the little opportunities that fly at us each day can have the biggest impact. Pay attention to Mother nature's nudge's, she'll protect you. Night.

### MrPinkies — 2013-12-12T21:26:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-the-little-things.8700/post-103238>

\*Tears\* awwwwwwwwwwwwwwesome!!!!!! xoxo

### caroline — 2013-11-09T00:56:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jks-latest-podcast-with-evav-nrand.8703/post-100208>

I really get so much out of the podcasts.... They are so great for us non- geeks. I must say tho .... I like my head hurting on a daily basis too ....self inflicted pain via jackkruse.com what's not to love.

Jack talked about some interesting things we hadn't heard before. His meditation room sounds awesome especially as it sounds like it is built for two....how lovely.

I am even more confused about transition metals now ... So a really dopey question.....Jack talked about using crystals in surgery ...... Crystals , with their transition metals, attract non native EMF - right?

So that means we are attracting EMF to us and the crystals are absorbing them? All of them coming our way?

So what I thought about my copper bracelet wasn't correct?? I thought I was attracting non native EMF to myself by the boatload .... And then becoming very dehydrated....

Is it the same for rings, earrings, necklaces etc? I am not thinking this thru correctly..... I need a grade 5 version I guess ......

Y'all.... What was your take home from this podcast? Do you have a special meditation place?

We need more details! Lots more.....

Wouldn't a meditation place outside be more beneficial?

### caroline — 2013-11-09T01:23:12+0200

I can't wait to meet you in person Martin .... One day soon..

But ......Jack talked about 2 dimensional relationships on the net...... say it isn't so Martin?

In my mind - you are beautiful and your mind/ heart are actively engaged to the nth degree.....and you are very 3D to me..... I know I am right about this.....

### caroline — 2013-11-09T01:39:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jks-latest-podcast-with-evav-nrand.8703/post-100217>

Let's talk about this .... Do we only see a person in 2 dimension on the net/FB etc....... Or over time .... Does a person's true self display itself?

I know I have experienced this personally .....someone from the net just wasn't who I thought they were. Are they actively downplaying their true personality? Or - is that just how it is?

I realize some people say rude, stupid, hurtful things on electronic media ..... But surely some of us are real??

Over time .... Do we become 3 dimensional? Surely after 3 years of this .....we would inadvertently display our true personality? Or am I full of crap? Head in the sand??

What about Skype - surely that is somewhat real?

But the touching thing is so important ..... When we transmit and receive energy.....when I am out everyday - I get the most glorious smile usually ..... But some people look away .....so much negative energy. Generally older men only give you a brief look or a nod..... But Jack talked about this in the sex webinar .....men communicate side by side.

Oddly - I find younger men are much better communicators. - why is that? They will often say something very pleasant.
And it is not as if they are hitting on me....I am old enough to be their Mother.....

Older men seem to almost be scared of life ....is that a fair characterization? I think I am too intense these days....

So - the takeaway for me ..... I need to hug you one day Martin.....apologies to your DW - who I know you adore!

### prAna303 — 2013-11-09T10:29:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jks-latest-podcast-with-evav-nrand.8703/post-100245>

> Do we only see a person in 2 dimension on the net/FB etc....... Or over time .... Does a person's true self display itself?

\- I think that the only way to make a person 3D is to be "live" with them. Deep inside us there is so many signals that must get their resonance otherwise we can not really connect. The problem with the net is that it is digital and based on digital ways of making the natural / analog things sendable, it that process we loose every little odd overtone, the crisp details, i would say the whole thing that makes a LIVING organism. In fact you could be speaking to a computer who makes answers based on some logarithm (think google). If you meet someone live and then connect thru the web you can get a memory from him or her but you will never sense the person right here and right now.

> I know I have experienced this personally .....someone from the net just wasn't who I thought they were. Are they actively downplaying their true personality? Or - is that just how it is?

\- The big problem today is that many live their lifes on the web, they can not sense their own ways of being anymore... So when someone talks out on the web you should always think twice about what they are saying. I mean, if you never been in a live situation where you hurt someones heart, how should you be able to sense the things we do not say in words?

> Over time .... Do we become 3 dimensional? Surely after 3 years of this .....we would inadvertently display our true personality? Or am I full of crap? Head in the sand??

\- Never i would say, the thing you can see is the persons way of acting over time. Does he or she behave in a regular way or do the words they say mean one thing today and another tomorrow, do they change standpoint over time without any good reason and so on.

> What about Skype - surely that is somewhat real?

\- In my life, ONLY if you have been seeing someone live then you can add that to the situation, it is not live but better than facebook. For me, the big problem once again, IT IS DIGITAL. Where are the small things that you can not see, smell but SENSE?

> Older men seem to almost be scared of life ....is that a fair characterization? I think I am too intense these days....

\- For me? I would say that many, both men and women, young and elder are scared. They are scared of real power and energy. When you spend your life wobbling thru life, scratching your iPhone you get a big disturbance, a big error in your system when you see pure energy. At first they find it exciting but pretty quick they tend to go the other way because they can not keep up with someone who is powerful. The world is as you are, when you have energy you transmit on a different plane than the other, because of that you must look for the ones that resonates with you, stop looking at the ones that will make you sad, look at the stars. But remember that everyone has a chance to become a star also, so you can not be rude to others because you feel that you have nothing in common with them, that is how the www works, not superheros.

### SeaHorse — 2013-11-09T15:13:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jks-latest-podcast-with-evav-nrand.8703/post-100257>

Wow.....Peter, that was a great meld of words and images. Circus is where art and fear come together...amazing.

Audrey...I will add to what Peter has written above....I think all the connections that we make online, through the forum, etc. have significance...of course they do, but they are not full relationships and what isn't there is what is most important ultimately. Just speaking of the forum, think how much knowledge we've gained reading it over the last few years....but that knowledge still has to be activated in order to have significance for us, and in our "friendships" too...I'm feel certain that we would have a wonderful time if we ever met, but think how little we know of each other apart from our own isolated words floating around in space. There is a lot of room for distortion. One of Don Miguel Ruiz five agreements is Don't make Assumptions. We need to be in the presence of a living body to really feel the essence of someone. Of course relationships exist in a spectrum and as I said, online friendships can be a significant, I just don't think they hold a candle to the experience of being with someone. I was taught that 90percent of communication is non-verbal.....so that gives me an idea of what I'm missing by just dealing with words. At the same time words are powerful, so we can't dismiss them in any way. Some people are verbal and can paint a picture of themselves that has nothing to do with reality....others are mostly mute and hold great truths that are only expressed through action....

I think in a perfect world we would be engaging in this healing path face to face with our teachers and our fellow travellers.....yet it is also true that our modern world has created many of our individual challenges, and we must use what is available to meet these challenges....so thanks be for the Dr. Kruse, the internet, magnetico and all of you!!

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-09T17:42:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jks-latest-podcast-with-evav-nrand.8703/post-100270>

It all comes down to what you treasure, in my opinion. Any treasure worth having, you have to dig for it. Gold is what most people 'pan' for. There's "gold" in people too, but sometimes you have to move a lot of dirt to find it. But it's always there, and it's worth digging for. This makes everyone worth treasuring. So how do you find that treasure? The greatest treasure map is found in our hearts. That is the place we should dig first and yet many don't have the courage to do it. They dig anywhere but in their hearts. Today find the gems in your life: passion, love, hope, desire, faith, strength, and knowledge. When "you know" what is buried in your heart no thief can steal anything from you........

# Thread: Pandora's box is open and I need to shut it :)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pandoras-box-is-open-and-i-need-to-shut-it.8716/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### thenursisin — 2013-11-10T02:58:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pandoras-box-is-open-and-i-need-to-shut-it.8716/post-100300>

Hi everyone:

My name is Donna and I am a registered nurse for over 34 years. I have always been healthy and naturally thin. I have run marathons and half marathons and always have been active.

In the last 20 years, I have developed Hashimoto's disease after my second pregnancy. With that I have had a partial thyroidectomy due to a huge growth and and fear of cancer. Thankfully, no cancer. I have been on synthroid, developed anxiety and insomnia and was placed on Paxil. As time progressed I developed skin issues, asthma, GERD, and huge digestive issues with a special reaction to WHEAT but no celiac per blood tests and colonoscopy. I have developed IBS. I have developed rosacea. I have developed severe ragweed/mite allergies. I have gained 40 lbs. No matter how much I exercise, I can't lose weight. I also have been in alcohol recovery for 8 years.

I switched to Paleo lifestyle and felt better but feel huge cravings to sweets and chocolates. I wondered if I wasn't just switching addictions. After several Whole 30s I felt better but couldnt stick to the plan on a constant basis. After several months of large carb/wheat binge eating, I have raised the white flag. I am **DONE**. I can't eat like this anymore and feel like this anymore. I receive acupuncture twice a week and have switched to walking/nordic walking/elliptical training and weight training twice per week. No more running.

I have begun to read Jack Kruse (EpiPaleo on Amazon) and feel immediately drawn to his writings and beliefs. Eureka!!!! I have stumbled upon a solution that might work for me. I want to get off of Paxil and lose the weight and feel like my pre Hashimotos self again. I have begun to take Probiotics for the last month and that has greatly helped me overall.

I need help and think this forum is a great start. Any advice would be greatly appreciated.....

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-10T16:16:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-largest-biologic-experiment-ever-done-on-humans.8719/post-100318>

You might ask why I believe this to be true? The answer is based in Maxwell's 4 laws of electromagnetism.

Time-varying magnetic fields will induce electric currents in the organism. Static magnetic fields, however, cannot induce currents, so how do they produce biological effects? Static fields affect organisms by their ability to orientate magnetically sensitive molecules and by deflecting endogenous electrical currents. ### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-10T16:18:13+0200

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-10T16:23:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-largest-biologic-experiment-ever-done-on-humans.8719/post-100320>

The answer goes back the Quantum electron blog post......of two yrs ago. [http://www.jackkruse.com/do-food-electrons-impart-a-quantum-effect/](http://www.jackkruse.com/do-food-electrons-impart-a-quantum-effect/)
And now you layer in what you just learned about transition metals...........Some molecules can orientate themselves in a magnetic field. Like water and biologic proteins with transition metals in them. Remember in QED electron pairs have spins that oppose one another. As a consequence of their electron spins, electrons behave as though they are **miniscule bar magnets** each possessing a magnetic moment, called the Bohr magneton, and is of the order of 10−23 J/Tesla. As I mentioned in the November 2013 webinar , the electrons in an atom are arranged in shells about a central nucleus. I told you about the D shell electrons being really special in transition metals in EE 7 and 8.

A complete electron shell always contains pairs of electrons with opposite spins. This is a quantum rule, not a Jack Kruse rule. Hence a complete shell has no net magnetic moment when the electrons are present because the spins oppose one another. In the transition metals such as iron, cobalt and nickel, there are unpaired electrons, **not only in the outer shell but also in an inner D shell.** In iron, for instance, five of the six electrons in the n = 3, l = 2 subshell have parallel spins, so that iron atoms have appreciable magnetic moments and are referred to as ferromagnetic. This is what makes these metals special for biology. These electrons become able to do some amazing things when they can freely float in a semiconductor lattice. Water and collagen are that lattice.

Transition metals are the only metals that can be magnetized. And on being magnetized, the domains of these electrons and in their respective shells, progressively align themselves until all are oriented in the same direction in parallel with the external field. This is called reducing entropy........because order is being brought to bear by the external magnetic field either on a star, the tectonic plate, or for biology it is the Earth's magnetic field.

SuperNova: Beyond a certain temperature, the atomic alignment within the electron domains disappears, and a ferromagnetic material becomes merely paramagnetic. The temperature at which this happens, the Curie point, is 770Â°C for iron. Well the temperature in the core of star that has burned all its gaseous fuel certainly exceeds 770 degrees C. And when this happens Nickel begins to emit energy from its atomic nucleus. This energy is due to negative beta decay of its D shell electrons. This is tied to the video from the Cal Berkley cosmologist I linked to in EE7 for those of you not following the details along......As the star continues to exhaust all fuel......the core of the star becomes iron and it begins to collapse in on itself due to the high temperatures. Here is where the Curie point matters. At extreme temperature or pressures all transition metals lose their atomic order and their electron domains begin to roam freely in the core of the star.........This creates massive nuclear energy in the form of electrons flying all over the place, but it also makes the inner core of the star very very chaotic. This is called an increase in entropy. November 2013 webinar alert. ....These emitted energized photons from these chaotic transition metals then begin to delocalize and create nuclear collisions to change the atomic structure of iron and nickel.....and the star explodes.......In the explosion the electrns cause atomic nuclei of the transition metals to fuse to make new elements........They make all the heavier and lighter metals most of you know about like Mg, Ca, gold, and platinum. This is the stardust we are made from. This also means that the information from that explosion is carried in those new elements and remains there until a cell can order and re energize the atomic nuclei once again in the new environment. On Earth, we use the photoelectric effect, water chemistry and the Earth magnetic field to create this set of requirements to regain order. (think entropy) Now go listen to the November 2013 webinar.............

OK now come back and read the rest of this.........thread.

Atoms and molecules with fewer unpaired electrons do not form electron domains at ordinary temperatures, they simply become paramagnetic or diamagnetic. A paramagnetic atom or molecule has a permanent dipole moment ( a magnet all the time) , and therefore tends to align itself in the direction of the applied field it finds itself in. A diamagnetic atom or molecule, on the other hand, has **no permanent dipole moment** ( means it can be magnetic when the field allows for it, but normally it is not)

When an applied magnetic field is added to the new environment of these atoms ( OK now think the transition metal proteins or water in you) This immediately affects the orbital motion of the electrons in such a way as to produce a magnetic moment in the opposite direction to the applied magnetic field. Remember Maxwell's 4 laws of electromagnetism show us that the electric and magnetic fields are at 90 degree angles to one another............This is why this can happen. An MRI takes full advantage of this aspect of these atoms in you.

Want to see where all this knowledge becomes really interesting...........for biology when you understand that the atomic physics and the action of electrons is primordial to all biochemistry? **_Pure samples of membrane lipids are known to align themselves in an external magnetic field._** Hey, did you know the human brain has more lipid membranes than any other organ? Think EE 5 now.......maybe stop and have a re read here.

OK next step in understanding:

It has been suggested that the effect of static magnetic fields (think Earth or magnetico but both do vary a small bit) on the permeability of lipid vesicles to drugs is due to the summation of diamagnetic alignments of large molecular aggregates to the external field occurring near membrane phase transition temperatures. I talked to you about phase transition temperatures when we spoke about water......a phase transition is gas to liquid and liquid to water etc.......all of these are coupled to what? TEMPERATURE

Guess what else biology does not yet realize? Proteins are also diamagnetic due to the planar peptide bonds, which, in the α-helix, in particular, are all aligned along the axis, and this will give rise to substantial diamagnetic anisotropy. Stop.......and re listen to the November 2013 webinar even before you look up what diamagnetic anisotropy means..........
Anisotropy is just another fancy word for making order from the atomic chaos naturally found. You can use many things to improve anisotropy and transition metals that become diamagnetic become ordered. Remember from our above lesson that a diamagnet is not a permanent magnet!!!! These actions are important.......as you will see as the blog continues on because diamagnetic atoms allow us to create Maxwell demons. (November 2013 webinar alert) This is why all biologic proteins have transition metals in them. They have the innate atomic ability to create order using no energy. This allows them to skate around the second law of thermodynamics when they are coupled in reactions. All biochemistry is coupled ironically. See Evolution really really really understands transition metals well. She rubbed off the print of the dollar bill when she used this trick in natures laws to create energy from nada. It turns out all conductors exhibit an effective diamagnetism when they experience a **changing magnetic field**. I told you we are made up of tons of different kinds of semiconductors. This means with the EArth's normal small varying magnetic field fluxes and currents can be made within those semiconductors. guess what else. With time these cycles (think circadian or thermohaline, or vortices) also become coupled......naturally without using energy, while continuing to bring order to a cell by reducing its entropy!!!!

How? The Lorentz force on electrons causes them to circulate around forming eddy currents. (think whirlpool, hurricane, tornando, think EE 4, and the Quantum sleep webinar when thinking of eddy currents.) Eddy current of delocalized electrons are small tiny vortices of electron energy that biology can use to never break the second law of thermodynamics. This is how life orders itself using the laws of nature you learned about in EE4. The electron "eddy currents", are a type of electric current. What did i teach you above about electric currents? They induce a magnetic field themselves at 90 degree angles. (think how the circle of willis acts in your brain from EE 5) Electron eddy currents then produce an induced magnetic field **opposite the applied field**, resisting the conductor's motion. What did i tell you about "resisting molecular motion" in the November 2013 webinar using Ling's experiments? What did EE 8 say? Does physics dictate biology? Short answer? Yep.

So diamagnetism is another example of a Maxwell demon. It gives order to atoms by giving it the property of being **directionally dependent**, as opposed to isotropy, which implies identical properties in all directions. Here you can see this is another fancy way to describe order and disorder with no transfer of energy required. It can be defined as a difference, when measured along different axes, in a material's physical or mechanical properties (absorbance, refractive index, conductivity, tensile strength, etc.) An example of anisotropy in nature is the light coming through a polarizer. (Think your sunglasses or better yet think about your retina or a chloroplast membrane (November 2013 webinar alert) And another example of anisotropy is found in ordinary wood we burn, which is easier to split along its grain than against it. ### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-10T17:59:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-largest-biologic-experiment-ever-done-on-humans.8719/post-100329>

Because an altered electromagnetic field has massive implications at the cell membrane because of the QED principles in this thread.
Proteins embedded in membrane lipids belong to a large class of molecules called liquid crystals. Water is the best example of a liquid crystal but there are tons of others in physics. Liquid metal superconductors like helium or hydrogen are great examples. Superconductors may be considered perfect diamagnets (χv = −1), since they NATURALLY expel all fields (except in a thin surface layer) due to the Meissner effect. (Taught you about the Meissner effect in EE 4, 5, and 6.)
To be clear, however, this effect is not due to electron eddy currents, as in ordinary diamagnetic materials. Water becomes a superconductor when it interacts with the proteins embedded in a lipid membrane.

Water chemistry: If a powerful magnet (Think your transition metal proteins in the lipid membrane) becomes covered with a layer of thin water (that is thin compared to the diameter of the magnet) then the field of the magnet **significantly repels the water**, naturally with no use of energy using QED. This action creates and exclusion zone. And exclusion zone is an empty space where no atoms exist. In this exclusion zone water begins to have some amazing properties that physicists and electrical engineer's take full advantage of for your modern tech gadgets. The simple proof that this happens naturally is that this action causes a slight dimple in the water's surface that may be seen by its reflection.

So water, protein and membrane lipids can exist in a number of mesophases, (phases that are neither solid nor liquid, but in between). These mesophases are characterised by long-range order in which all the molecules are aligned in quasicrystalline arrays based upon the field of action they are in......... Liquid crystals are relatively easily aligned with electric and magnetic fields, which is the basis of the liquid crystal display screens that come with modern TV's, Iphones and Ipads, watches, laptop computers and every computer game made today. Biology does the same thing with with different substances........you just don't realize it yet but you will. (Novemeber 2013 webinar alert)

Another key point that is quite significant, alternating magnetic fields invariably fail to produce the ‘twisted’ morphology in these flies, but many other abnormalities were produced in large numbers. this is why Tesla went mad in case your keeping track folks...........he suffered from neuroimmune decline due to depression and bipolar disorder because of all the AC current he passed thru himself in the electric wars with Edison and JP Morgan.

# Thread: How microwave energy heats water.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-microwave-energy-heats-water.8721/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### CindyB — 2013-11-10T18:46:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-microwave-energy-heats-water.8721/post-100331>

Currently reading "Cross Currents" from Becker. He references a Scientific American article written for "The Amateur Scientist" column by Jearl Walker. Found the article on-line here:

[http://jesseenterprises.net/amsci/1987/02/1987-02-fs.html](http://jesseenterprises.net/amsci/1987/02/1987-02-fs.html)

"THE SECRET OF A MICROWAVE oven's fast action is that water in the food rapidly absorbs the energy of the waves. At first thought this is surprising. In most cases where a material absorbs light or other types of electromagnetic radiation there must be a resonant match between the energy of the radiation and the energy changes possible in the atoms and molecules of the material. Water absorbs infrared radiation resonantly, but the frequency of microwaves is far too low for a resonant match. How, then, is energy transferred from the microwaves to the random thermal motion of the molecules of water? Several hypotheses I shall discuss represent a groping toward the answer. First one needs a bit of background on the physics of microwaves and the water molecule (H2O), consisting of two atoms of hydrogen and one atom of oxygen."​

Understanding how imparting (thermal) motion can happen with 2.45 GHz irradiation helps me understand how other frequencies (e.g. harmonics, et.al.) might impart change on cellular chemo-dynamics (e.g. diffusion, stearic hindrance, surface adhesion, etc.)

This jesseenterprises.net website is a treasure trove of articles explaining deep science to the amateur scientist. Fun!

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-10T19:47:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-microwave-energy-heats-water.8721/post-100334>

everything in that link is in the Thread I made this AM on biologic coherence in the doctors forum ironically. Must be quantum biology at work. I describe precise how it happens........

So I was searching for something else and then this comment on EMF8 hit me so much, I had to share. Love these snippets.....I mean bombs.

Carbs do not hold water.......they cause us to dehydrate. This is why glucose laden drinks stimulate thirst. Since carbs are ATP oor foods most come packed in their evolutionary packages with a high water contents to offset their lack of electron power. This is why carbs grow in longer light cycles when the photons from the sun are most powerful........to draw us out into the sun to acquire the fruit so we can gain directly from the photoelectric effect. Today humans have radically disturbed that balance. If the water is treated or fluoridated the water is essentially useless to a biologic system and will be urinated out and not used to rehydrate our collagen carbon nanotubes. When this happens we also lose magnesium because mag is hydrophillic as many people seem to have forgotten. This is another reason why people who eat too many carbs, like a T2D, become Mg depleted and dehydrate easy. The fluoride is a dielectric to DHA in neurons and displaces iodine in the brain's cell membranes and especially at synapses and it causes avalanche collapse of the semiconducting current. This is why it screws our ability to water superconduct. Re read Brain gut 12 and the top ten paleo supplement blog post carefully.......the info is there and the next few blogs are going to open that discussion more. ~ Jack Kruse

[http://www.jackkruse.com/emf-8-quantum-bone/](http://www.jackkruse.com/emf-8-quantum-bone/)

And why they have some many injuries from their workouts. Their collagen can not handle the loads without the water bound properly.

# Thread: Carbs in broth?

**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jasmin — 2013-11-11T08:25:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carbs-in-broth.8730/post-100405>

Hope that this is not a stupid question, but when I make broth I use bones from grassfed animals or the bones and heads from fish and I use carrots celery, garlic, onions and herbs for flavor. I remove the vegetables when the broth is finish, but is the carbs from the vegetables now in the broth???

### yewwei.tan — 2013-11-11T10:38:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carbs-in-broth.8730/post-100408>

Yup. Assuming you boil the broth for a long time, the broth ends up tasting sweet, and I suspect that any soluble carbohydrates would get into the broth (in the case of onions, probably half the total carbohydrate).

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-11T14:47:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carbs-in-broth.8730/post-100418>

i do the same thing.

### Jasmin — 2013-11-15T09:15:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carbs-in-broth.8730/post-100765>

I boil the broth for a long time and it taste sweet - I will have to keep track with the amount of vegetables and then calculate with half the total carbs.
Thank you so much

# Thread: WAPF President Sally Fallon on Robb Wolf.......interesting.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wapf-president-sally-fallon-on-robb-wolf-interesting.8736/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-11T21:29:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wapf-president-sally-fallon-on-robb-wolf-interesting.8736/post-100447>

I have no idea why it took Sally 3 years to review this book. There must be some trouble in paleo paradise with WAPF members. I think most of what she says in here can be interpreted as sour grapes but I think she is spot on when it comes to her complaints about the Vitamin A issue in the brain and what she pointed out about fish oil. While she correctly pointed out the dietary decoupling she whiffed badly on the real paleo problem with Vitamin a recycling in the brain, non native EMF and blue light destroy the photoperiod and this is a double whammy to those who do not realize it. Robb has said on his podcast he may have overdone his rec's on fish oil but interestingly in later printings of the book it still has not be removed.I also think calling it the original human diet shows complete lack of understanding of neurobiology and its evolution. A refresher course on Dr. Cunnane and Crawford's work should have eliminated that from the title because you can not have a human brain without a ton of seafood. Grass fed meat is not that ticket.

I personally think Ms. Fallon is going to create a war with Robb's followers. Paleo people are quite harsh when you attack one of their leaders, even when the criticism is worth a discussion.

The timing sure is odd.

[http://www.westonaprice.org/thumbs-down-reviews/the-paleo-solution-byrobb-wolf](http://www.westonaprice.org/thumbs-down-reviews/the-paleo-solution-byrobb-wolf)

### Danco3636 — 2013-11-12T01:45:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wapf-president-sally-fallon-on-robb-wolf-interesting.8736/post-100468>

Interesting find....

### smart cat — 2013-11-22T16:22:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wapf-president-sally-fallon-on-robb-wolf-interesting.8736/post-101354>

I believe Sally Falloms war agaist the Paleio Community is becaue WAPF is not very succesful in attracting new members while Paleo is becoming more and more popular. It's about market share I think.

Here is a nice blogpost with lots of comments about the debate:

Why Weston Price and Paleo Will Not Survive Without Each Other
