**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lustigs-new-video-extremely-bad.8743/post-100484>

I was willing to give him rope after his first video.......it was good but he made a lot of mistakes in it by leaving out leptin.........then in this one he adds it and completely botches it BADLY. He is using this fructose, to get a political solution using horrible science.

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ceFyF9px20Y#t=4413](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ceFyF9px20Y#t=4413)

All of slides can be explained by many other factors.......like EMF's effect on the hypothalamus and the inner mitochondrial membrane. When he showed the fat kid with the brain tumor who was irradiated and said the neurons were missing I could not handle it. X-rays are high energy EMF's. Geopathic stressors of the Middle East countries........he just misses the boat. Causal medical inference? WOW. He needs a serious dose of physical chemistry and QED. The pathways he shows all use proteins with transition metals at every step. Every hormone he mentions does too..........A lot of what he says is true but the conclusions he draws.........does not support the the things he claims are true.

When he mentions stress.......and then FB........dopamine. WOW he is brain stem clueless..........

At AHS 2011 after hearing him speak live, I was concerned about some of the incorrect things he said about leptin. In this talk he goes even deeper into the mess. I came away saying we need to give him some rope to see where he is really headed.........in this video he shows us he is 100% completely this generations Ancel Keys..........really really disappointed in where he is going. Dr. Feinman pegged him correctly in 2011. He was sure but I was not. Now I am too.

I am no fan of evidence based medicine but this talk is exceptionally bad in trying to peel the onion skin back to find the truth............

### Martha Ray — 2019-07-24T05:15:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-in-cars.8744/post-274909>

Interesting tread. I am driving a ’99 CRV ‘Silver Cord’ that I love and my DH is driving a white ’10 Hybrid Ford Fusion ‘Snowball’. I name cars and computers. We have lighter colored cars because we believe they are safer due to better visibility. DH loves the gas mileage of the Hybrid but I’ve wondered about it with my EMF sensitivity. Strangly as a passenger it seems to charge me up but when driving it I do not feel as energized. As Jack Kruse mentioned “**the amount of EMF present is very close in a car cockpit.”** My DO doctor said he has noticed issues with some of his patients--some drain and some charge. He feels it is good for me…but I am not so sure. Also feel it may be a factor with DH bouts of Afib. He got the car, had cataract surgery and our little 15 yr Italian Greyhound all passed shortly before his Afib attacks began 5 years ago.

I’ve wanted a Ford Flex for about 5 years. I rented one on one of my annual trips to Texas and felt it was more of a vacation than the trip… and I loved the trip. I felt so safe in Houston traffic, even in San Antonio and Austin where I don’t know the roads nearly as well. On trips I depend on a Garmin brand GPS… guess they are all the same kinda bad.

I have been examining most everything with the lens of ‘is this healthy’ for 10 years… a challenge for sure and discouraging at times but convinced that it is worthwhile!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-in-cars.8744/post-286647>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=286634)

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=286634)

> I tried a Volkswagen Polo, 2016, because I want a smaller car. It was in great shape. But its magnetic radiation was HORRIBLE. No thanks. Polos are out.
> Then I tried 2 different Kia\`s, one Kia Rio, and another smaller variant of Kia. The smallest I think. They were both about 2016/2014, quite similar aged as the Polo. But they were so much better radiation wise, way way better. I would buy one of these. Kia or Hyundai (had Hyundai i30 before my recent car Opel Vectra, that was a great car too, not much radiation at all!), they seem to be a great choice

**[@Awainer1](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/3233/), [@ElectricUniverse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/21203/) and Inger,** (I'm not sure how to copy others in on conversations? Saw others using @ with name )

### ElectricUniverse — 2020-02-23T17:29:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-in-cars.8744/post-286654>

> [Martha Barger said:](/goto/post?id=286647)
>
> **[@Awainer1](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/3233/), [@ElectricUniverse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/21203/) and Inger,** (I'm not sure how to copy others in on conversations? Saw others using @ with name )
>
> **Do you think it matters where you test the cars ? and what meter do you use to test? I don't even own a meter yet?**
> **May not be able to find out if one tire is better than another?**
> **... I used to ride horses would like to go back to them.**

You make a good point-- it definitely could matter a great deal where you are testing the vehicle.

Unless you live under a rock in the remote outback you are always exposed to varying levels of ambient nnEMF. That is likely to confound any reliable meter measurements in and around a vehicle (applies especially to MW/RF radiation these days).

### Inger — 2020-02-23T18:28:39+0200

Yes sure. I had to test drive near my home and test again. And at the car store there were high pulsed RF that were not from the car, because as we shut down the motor they were still present. And when I was close to my home those RF were not there anymore, even when car was running. So yes, it is important to drive around a little and try at different remote places away from celltowers.

### Dan2 — 2020-07-31T03:44:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-in-cars.8744/post-291276>

Mahindra Roxor

[https://www.google.com/search?q=mahindra+roxor&ie=utf-8&oe=utf-8&client=firefox-b-ab](https://www.google.com/search?q=mahindra+roxor&ie=utf-8&oe=utf-8&client=firefox-b-ab)

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mahindra\_Thar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mahindra_Thar)
"On March 2, 2018, Mahindra Automotive North America unveiled a variant of the Thar for United States market called the **[Roxor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mahindra_Roxor)** at its assembly plant in [Auburn Hills, Michigan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Auburn_Hills,_Michigan). It is assembled from [complete knock-down](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Knock-down_kit) kits. Unlike the Thar, the Roxor is not street legal, and is positioned as a [side-by-side](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Side_by_Side_%28UTV%29) [off-road vehicle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Off-road_vehicle) with a top speed of 45 mph. The Roxor is powered by a 2.5L turbodiesel, four-cylinder engine producing 62 hp and 144 lb-ft of torque, mated to a five-speed manual transmission. An automatic transmission is available as an option. It is only available with two seats, a 148-inch body length, and a 96-inch wheelbase.[\[2\]"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mahindra_Thar#cite_note-2)

Specs
[https://d3lwr7t4tsy765.cloudfront.net/Specs/ROXOR-2020MY-SpecSheet.pdf](https://d3lwr7t4tsy765.cloudfront.net/Specs/ROXOR-2020MY-SpecSheet.pdf)
No anti-lock brakes, says top speed 55 mph here, 144 ft lbs torque, 3490 lb towing capacity, max towing speed 15 mph. Maybe not the best for taking a camper on a road trip.
Starting price MSRP $16,599 US

How to make it street legal
[https://teamroxor.com/threads/what-is-needed-to-street-legal-a-mahindra-roxor.5/](https://teamroxor.com/threads/what-is-needed-to-street-legal-a-mahindra-roxor.5/)

How to remove the speed limiter so it can go 70 mph+
[https://www.dirtlegal.com/blog/2018...-your-mahindra-roxor-and-unlock-its-top-speed](https://www.dirtlegal.com/blog/2018/7/14/heres-how-to-derestrict-your-mahindra-roxor-and-unlock-its-top-speed)

### nicld — 2013-11-13T16:28:39+0200

I thought it was EXTREMELY helpful.

Did not really know the difference between Ox bile and Betatine HCL and that my pregnenolone was in the crapper. Now to dig deep and find information on that and what to do to get it better. I am going to hold of on trying supplements till I get my next set of blood tests ran, like Audrey is saying. The more I heard about Misty's issues the more I am thinking that I am dealing with some of the same stuff.

Can't wait till the thyroid one next week. Going to revisit stuff and have some question ready.

### tellmisty — 2013-11-13T22:17:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-with-dr-tim-jackson-bootcamp.8756/post-100663>

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=100626)
>
> I thought it was EXTREMELY helpful.
>
> Did not really know the difference between Ox bile and Betatine HCL and that my pregnenolone was in the crapper. Now to dig deep and find information on that and what to do to get it better. I am going to hold of on trying supplements till I get my next set of blood tests ran, like Audrey is saying. The more I heard about Misty's issues the more I am thinking that I am dealing with some of the same stuff.
>
> Can't wait till the thyroid one next week. Going to revisit stuff and have some question ready.

I have so many "pieces" that are off. But nothing is egregiously off -- to mainstream docs. That's what's been so frustrating. I'm seeing an integrated medicine doc, and even HE didn't really seem keen on running labs and digging deeper, because I do have some labs that seem pretty good and I don't have major symptoms. I just have a body that's mutating -- thyroid nodules, endometriosos, left ovary removed (those are the biggies). And docs give me these diagnosis with an accompanying, "And all your labs are normal! "

Makes me want to punch them in the throat.

So all along, my hunch has been: Something's not right with my thyroid. Something's not right with my hormones. Something's off with my liver.

And having serum labs was just ... kind of confirming something wasn't right, but it was reading the tea leaves, ya know? So I supplement, get rid of EMFs, have water delivered, do the orange glasses, eat squeaky clean ... and I just feel like those things keep my labs looking good enough that docs think everything is okay.

The Genova Complete Hormone Panel shattered the glass ceiling for me, thank God! I now have a REAL picture of what is effed up. And when Dr. Tim started breaking down for me why my cortisol was so high, and how it affected everything else, and nothing was going to get better until I figured out what the stressors were, that was HUGE. He went through my 23andme test and helped me to understand what all that crap meant (!!!) and how I needed to supplement to support methylation and a whole host of other genetic things. According to Dr Tim, until I get rid of the immune stressors, I won't be able to fix the other problems. He's mapping all of this out for us in the Heal Your Hormones Bootcamp.

I can't even put into words how freaking ECSTATIC I am to finally have a picture of what the f\*\*\* is wrong with me so I can get about the business of righting the ship. His sequence is awesome ... makes total sense, and is such a compliment to what Jack's been teaching us for the last few years.

As a side note, once I had these labs and started to understand precisely where I was positioned in this health labyrinth, I started combing through the blog and re-reading Jack's post. I was able to actually start to fit these posts around my own situation, which was so huge. I feel like the puzzle went from black-and-white to technicolor ... and we aren't done! I'm still waiting on my GI Effects test to come back, and I've had several titers ran. Finding out which viruses are high and getting more clarity on what the plan of attack should be.

I'm really pushing Dr. Tim to create a "manual" to accompany the 23andme / MTHFR results so people can reference it when they get their MTHFR reports back. That would be SO helpful to people.

He's really brilliant at helping you put your plan together ... he's got the sequencing down, which is helpful because going through the blog and trying to piece together your own plan is daunting, and we can really spin our wheels and waste a lot of $$$.

### caroline — 2013-11-13T22:38:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/webinar-with-dr-tim-jackson-bootcamp.8756/post-100666>

So well said Misty.......Dr. K. has been telling us for years to get our labs done and then go from there! I really liked Dr. Tim's approach - his sequencing........makes sooooo much sense.

Just the fact that he said he assumes almost every one one has a leaky gut - that is where we should start....don't even bother testing - until later if you need it.

He is giving us so much detail ..... but he is easy to understand- it is hard to remember it all. I definitely need multiple listens ...and I need more hours in the day!

When one things clicks - it leads us to another path sometimes that we hadn't considered or we just overlooked .... and, as you said, it leads us back to blogs that we didn't relate to the first couple of times.....

This is going to be an amazing bootcamp .......I can't even imagine the time y'all have put into this. Thankyou for arranging this for us...what an incredible opportunity.......

### seanb4 — 2013-11-15T21:27:52+0200

### Shijin13 — 2013-11-16T01:03:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-quantum-health-model.8760/post-100797>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=100783)
>
> Like the idea of visualising some of these concepts but I'm not sure I understand the diagram. Are the black arrows meant to be things that negatively effect becoming optimal?

Sean

They're inputs - things that can help you get to optimal - think nutrition = epipaleo or it could kick you out of optimal think food = wheat. Or think of a flu virus, or bacterial infection - that could easily knock you out - impacting your gut health and causing a stress response.

EMF does the same thing. not all inputs are good, not all inputs are bad - but they can cause stress upon you. Think about viruses - and go back and read Brain gut series. Viruses can help us evolve or they can kill us - and our gut is the mechanism by which we assimilate them everything is connected.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-16T04:43:02+0200

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-16T04:51:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-quantum-health-model.8760/post-100804>

I hope you all listen to the Nov webinar.......i found it simple to do because we focused on one small aspect that is foundational to a quantum cell........The quantum cell takes the parts of nature.......you all learned in EE 4 and it adds in the protein back bone.......from these four fundamental things, acting in unison, or coherently, we get life.

This life.......is designed to be a see a saw........this is a synonym for meta stability. It can go up or down based upon what the cell faces in the environment it is put into..........

Randy just explained how the Quantum physics issue plays its roll of the dice on DNA: He said, "And don't forget, all of these interactions are quantum and therefore probabilistic and **not predictable. it just depends**. So the outcomes may be different--that is, one person may be healthy and another contracts cancer. We are all different because of our quantum nature." That captures the essence of the physics and yet explains why biology will never get it........because they think life is concrete when at its core.......it is quantum and it can never be pinned down ever.......just like an electron.

# Thread: Quantum physics proves that there IS an afterlife

**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Huck — 2013-11-15T13:39:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quantum-physics-proves-that-there-is-an-afterlife.8769/post-100771>

-   Robert Lanza claims the theory of biocentrism says death is an illusion
-   He said life creates the universe, and not the other way round
-   This means space and time don't exist in the linear fashion we think it does
-   He uses the famous double-split experiment to illustrate his point
-   And if space and time aren't linear, then death can't exist in 'any real sense' either

> He continued: 'Life is an adventure that transcends our ordinary linear way of thinking. When we die, we do so not in the random billiard-ball-matrix but in the inescapable-life-matrix.'

[http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencet...ics-proves-IS-afterlife-claims-scientist.html](http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencetech/article-2503370/Quantum-physics-proves-IS-afterlife-claims-scientist.html)

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-15T15:56:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quantum-physics-proves-that-there-is-an-afterlife.8769/post-100773>

space/time do not exist......they are manifestations of the maxwell demon's in us.........Nov 2013 webinar alert

### nonchalant — 2013-11-15T17:08:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quantum-physics-proves-that-there-is-an-afterlife.8769/post-100775>

I believe our primal kitty almost died Monday night. We saw that she had killed and eaten half of a rat that Saturday. It may have been poisoned, but I'm not sure why it would take so long to make her sick. To our relief, she was better Tuesday morning, and on Thursday she was completely back to normal. Good thing we perceived her as alive Tuesday morning.

This is so bizarre.

### nonchalant — 2013-11-17T03:22:41+0200

# Thread: Thread for non Geeks....... and those who love non Geeks!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 39
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2013-11-17T10:10:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100886>

I often have trouble with all the science..... anyone else? I want to get much, much better at this.

If JK is going thru all the trouble to lay all this out for us - I want to give it my best shot!

If you have something you don't understand ..... let's talk and see if we can figure it out together....unless I am a party of one????

Randy has given a really good explanation of transition metals in another thread....

C/T#7 is a great back to basics blog - in my opinion ..... what do y'all think?

Any ideas on how we can nail this????

### prAna303 — 2013-11-17T10:56:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100893>

For me the science is quite easy to understand BUT i can not spread the word in these terms. The geeky words is not for me to use, i can not talk that language and if i should try, i would look and behave like a fool i feel. So for me i take the science, go outside, look at the ocean, the mountains, the snowfall and connect the dots. When i get back i can speak the ideas in MY words so i can stand for those thoughts. When i get confused i always ask myself this:

How would i, we humans do IF we did not have all this weird things. How would i act if i was as wild as an animal?

### Inger — 2013-11-17T11:04:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100895>

Yeah.. I am like Peter too. I understand it because I feel/experience it in the real world. That is enough for me.  and I look up stuff and read about it if I get confused. I do not have a geek mind either. Must be because I grew up on potatoes. But I am not too sorry because of it, because I really enjoy life here and now. you do not need a geek brain to do that

I would say.. do not get stuck if something seems to complicated in some blogs, just read further and take the whole picture and live it. You will know

### SeaHorse — 2013-11-17T13:43:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100897>

Audrey....I've been meaning to ask you this for awhile and now you've opened the door....what exactly aren't you understanding? Because I see you changing your life and trying to live all the protocols and find your own way in the world.... what worries you if you can't repeat the exact steps of protein unfolding, or the methylation cycle or interesting behaviors of electrons and photons.

Because that knowledge is out there (or here, actually) doesn't mean you need to be able to memorize it. Understand it yes, in terms of how you are living your life....but it seems to me it is causing you a lot of stress which is not the point. And one thing we did learn very clearly and simply is if cortisol is off then a lot of other things are off....I don't feel that we have to know every element of the science to know what we need to do...

On the other hand, if this is a passion for you to know all this stuff at the same level that Dr. K writes about it, then "fill your boots" as they say in Newfoundland, but why stress about it? We all spend too much time on our computers reading the site as it is.....I'm not trying to be argumentative....You sound stressed to me....many of us are not going to be able to write out the science, but I can tell somebody why I am doing all the things I'm doing and you, of all of us who post seem to be on a mission to spread the word and it sounds like you do an amazing job.

Also, information overload is a killer when it comes to inspiration....there has to be a balance....."knowing better" also means knowing when information isn't the point and action and change is....something Dr. K has talked about a lot...an idea without execution etc. etc. So....that's my little loving poke in your side today Audrey....you're the best!!!

### Huck — 2013-11-17T13:46:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100898>

For about a year, I really made an effort to understand the blog posts. But I'm afraid my brain hasn't healed enough yet to understand the detail, so now I just skim the posts looking for the big picture idea and the specific ideas that are in bold. I can't hold enough information in my short term memory to understand complicated subjects for which I don't have an adequate knowledge base. Understanding the detail doesn't necessarily speed up the healing, so I don't sweat the details.

I keep waiting for the Google translator to add Krusean.

### Hemming — 2013-11-17T14:10:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100899>

I think Jack really wants to spread the message but his blogs get way too technical for the majority of the readers which scares away many. I listen a lot more to the podcasts than the recent blogs. Some of the earlier blogs were easier to understand. If you don't have an education in biology/medicine etc. you're screwed in some of them. I'm not saying that's right or wrong but more that there is a theoretical and practical side of this. I understand when people say its all about light, water and magnetism but how exactly do I live by those rules if I can't decipher it from a blog post?

### prAna303 — 2013-11-17T16:16:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100903>

My take on the form on the blogs and the forum is that, if you should get people hooked you must have the "for dummies" AND "geeky" sides together...

It is like a perfect game of flirting... You get a balance between action, thoughts and connection. The thing is that when you get stuck on some parts, it will take some action from you to get more information by yourself, you begin to act and not react.

So i think Jack is doing a magic game of flirting with us, and he does it really good.

### Inger — 2013-11-17T16:40:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100905>

Yes, the flirting thing is it! I am totally hooked since the very beginning... haha...

I think Jack is the flirting master

Hemming, I am very un-geeky but I just look up terms I do not understand if I feel I need it to get the picture he is painting. I even use google translator at times because my English is not the best... I think it is possible for anyone... but sure there must be a will to learn. Maybe... we must first feel the spirit.. and then we get hooked... like Peter describes it with the flirting thing. Then nothing is difficult anymore, because you are in love. Anyone been in deep love know the wings and magic it gives

Maybe it is all about love in the end

### Hemming — 2013-11-17T20:41:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100912>

C/T #7 was a GREAT one for me and how important TIMING is and the reasons why. Have always heard not to eat after 7 but never why and what it does to your hormones. Still trying to figure out what is going on when I wake up in the middle of the night and so I will reread this one multiple times.

### caroline — 2013-11-18T01:25:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100924>

Thankyou everyone for all your profound thoughts on this. All of you challenge me in so many ways and I thank you for that.

Initially the reason I started this thread is because I worry that newbies are embarrassed to ask questions that they perceive as stupid .... I know I did - and still do sometimes.

Jack has said if we don't ask questions - he doesn't know what we don't know ... and I appreciate that.

He told me once - long ago - about a dopey question .... "it isn't dopey - the dopey thing is that you waited so long to ask".

I always try to remember that and pass that along to new people. I hate to see them drop of - for whatever reason ....but especially if they are frustrated with the science. Overall - the concepts are simple to implement and then as we keep reading and asking questions we start connecting the dots.

As I have said, I really do appreciate that Dr. Kruse has to write for the science geeks and add in all the cites. If he doesn't he gets ripped to shreds and not taken seriously.

Seahorse - thankyou for your reply.....as you rightly point out - I do seem to be stressed and can't shake it off. I think I need to have some fun!!

There are so many pearls of wisdom in your post that really mean alot to me. I really do know all that but sometimes get a little off track!

All of you are my straight edge.........

I think what bothers me the most is - I can't explain things to people properly ...... using all the correct terminology.
I am going to stop worrying about that and just do what I am doing.....

I still think tho - if there is a concept we aren't grasping ....we should talk it thru. Randy gave such a great explanation for transition metals and, of course, Gretchen is always a help!

If I had waited to get the biology etc. I would still be at the starting gate - or worse - went elsewhere.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-18T03:00:08+0200

### cantweight — 2013-11-18T03:42:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100932>

In the real world i am geekier than most, on this site I am definitely a non-geek. I love reading these blogs and watching the webinars it is so raw and honest. I am so sick of medical bullshit that fully explainable science is refreshing whether I get it or not. No smoke blowing....it is what it is. While I dont get everything, what I have gotten has excited me and made me look at things differently; its given me hope

Something funny....a few months back I had an extra credit assignment for a history class I was taking. The assignment was very simple just share a website that correlated with a chapter in our history book. Our first chapter was all pre history, evolution "Lucy" and "Ardi"...so I shared Jacks site. I thought the bipedalism blog and a few others were a great match.

I got a zero. No points. I contacted the teacher to plead my case and ask why he gave me no credit....he said because no one in the class could possibly understand anything on the site.

So I figure no matter how much I dont get....I'm still getting more than my history professor....lol

### nonchalant — 2013-11-18T04:06:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100934>

I took a few Coursera classes online, but I found that I had problems with some of their initial premises. They would define the problem as pertaining to X, and then go merrily on their way, discussing all the ramifications of X. But I wanted to explore Y. But Y was dismissed early on, perhaps because Y didn't fit all those nifty equations they had cleverly figured out.

And since Jack was speeding things up on his blog, I just didn't have time to spend on elegant theory that didn't necessarily fit my observations.

The 'degree of slowness' of your Ferrari is directionally proportional to the intensity of memory in your brain. The better the hormones the better the memory. The degree of speed is directionally proportional to the intensity of forgetting. The more your circadian cycles are off the less time you have. When you follow QED, you travel "on light". But not at the same speed at sunlight. Everything in the world is about sex except sex. The reason they cars and hormones are bed fellows is because sex and speed is about power. Night

Education is the knowledge is the only thing that increases your wellness quotient. Why do I get "feisty" at times? Nobody will ever figure out what life is really all about, and it doesn't matter, honestly. But we are put here, on Earth, to completely explore the world with our curiosity and imagination. If you do not, can't, or won't I have very little regard for that line of thinking. You have a duty to you. Nearly everything is really interesting if you go into it deeply enough. Sadly, most people never fulfill their own thirst of their own curiosity and that pisses me off when I see it. Your glass of water should be emptied in your gut every minute of every day. Mother nature's imagination is infinitely greater than ours, so you need to realize quickly she's never going to let us relax. That stressor should get you off your behind to act. If it does not.........it's on you.

### nicld — 2013-11-18T19:51:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100972>

I think I am going to take reading and learning from the blogs the same way I did in college and I got my two degrees.

Each one will be copied into a word document and any words I do not get, I will look up and put their meaning in the document. Then, hopefully at the end of each blog I will try to write myself a summary of what I think it means. I need to get this stuff, my life and my families well being depends on it.

I may have started with one of the hardest series (Quantum Biology) but if I do it a little at a time, some might sink in. I have chemisty and two semesters of Physics in college but I think that all of that learning was left in the classroom right after the final exam. I just do not use it enough to remember it. So time to dust off the cobwebs and go back to school at the college of health with professor Dr. Kruse.

### nicld — 2013-11-18T21:07:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100976>

Oh, I don’t think I will “get” quantum mechanics but I at least need to understand the words and the basic meaning of them so I can get the basic jist of it.

For me, I need each one of the terms broken down so that it makes sense to me, kinda like a glossary of terms. I am a very written word learner more than a pure visual or oral word learner. I used to take notes from a lecture then rewrite them and color code them so they made sense to me. That’s how my crazy brain works and one can never go wrong with learning.

### caroline — 2013-11-19T00:39:08+0200

I think we all intuitively know this ... don't we?

I am going to stop worrying about newbies so much .... it is their job to read and ask questions, their own personal responsibility to get where they need to go and do what they need to do.......it is a journey of "1"

### caroline — 2013-11-19T00:58:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100987>

Yesterday, on a coach to Sydney, There was a really huge obese young woman. She fully took up the whole compartment/space between her seat and the one in front. She had her own seat right back and, sad to say, she looked like a beached whale splayed out.

Instead of feeling indignant about her plight and wondering why she doesn't help herself ....... I felt so sorry that she has been failed by her doctor, family, friends, us et all.

But, after reading this thread ....... It is her personal responsibility to somehow help herself ...... that is what all of us are doing ....that is what Dr. K. did.

If she isn't sick and tired of being sick and tired - what can anyone do.......

### Inger — 2013-11-19T14:43:27+0200

### caroline — 2013-11-17T10:24:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-shirt.8781/post-100887>

In one of JK's last podcasts - he talks about wearing a compression shirt. That surprised me that he still does that ......Does anyone have a take on that? I understand it compresses the fat cells - is there a detail we have missed? For me - that part of the podcast was really breaking up so I might have missed something critical!

Usually Jack prefers no clothes....... but I guess one can't make rounds starkers!

I have worn a compression like tank top for the past 18 months....I wonder if this is part of the reason my shape has changed dramatically? I just do it because I got used to wearing one and now love it - a lot better than undies ....sorry TMI ....

The only drawback .... if someone needed to undress me in a hurry......

### prAna303 — 2013-11-17T10:50:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-shirt.8781/post-100890>

Could it be something like this?

When you have been in a bad EMF place you must take some extreme actions to lower the entropy in the unit called you. Cold water AND pressure simulates the great depth of the oceans where life is strong and energy filled. So simply said, the shirts helps keeping the energy inside you like a closed system, stopping you to loose energy to the world around us.

### Inger — 2013-11-17T10:56:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-shirt.8781/post-100892>

Audrey...lol you are priceless. I know why I love you  (my secret tho..)

The compression shirt is because it makes less blood flow at the skin surface which makes it cool faster and stay colder as we do CT. The purpose with CT is to get the skin temp down. Not the core temp. That is how I have understood it.

I like Peters theory too  kind of could think about it like this, the blood get "pressured" into us from the surface = core temp stays warm better

### Butters — 2013-11-18T12:24:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everyone.8787/post-100947>

Hello Everyone,

I'm a 23-year-old student from Germany and have been linked to the Paleo/Low-Carb community for 2 years now.

One week ago I stumbled over the blog and since then I had so many aha-moments, it's unbelievable.

I'm studying sports science, so the weight is not my problem, but i'm very interested in the whole CT- and Performance-Thing and longevity. Plus seasonal-eating makes perfect sense to me.

I don't want to feel cold in winter anymore, be sick or weak now and in future and make the best out of my genes. So I start right now.

Greetings form the country of bread and potatoes

### sooperb — 2013-11-18T14:39:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everyone.8787/post-100949>

Hello Butters, I imagine you're going to be blown over by all the stuff in here. I have been around a couple of months and there is just so much to take in that I am never going to get through it. It is great to be among like minded people who don't laugh at the way you choose to live or mutter under their breath that you're strange or just plain mad  and who have been there and tried "it" and are willing to feed back on the good and the bad. I hope you stick around and tell us about your experiences.

### nonchalant — 2013-11-20T16:34:57+0200

Dr. K keeps referencing CT7. Could it be that with fewer resources (warm adaptation), the body has to cycle some hormones more? If energy were more abundant (cold adapted) perhaps prolactin or progesterone would have a bit more steady supply? Of course melatonin and cortisol are mutually exclusive, but what about other hormones? And would autophagy be confined to a two or three hour window in a cold-adapted human? Or might it occur during any time of relaxation? Would cortisol have more dips during the day, with a lower baseline curve? Perhaps our vortices would reverse more often during the day.

### QuantumRadical — 2013-11-21T04:48:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-november-webinar.8803/post-101206>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=101180)
>
> I love, love, love this webinar! I haven't gotten all the way thru yet.
>
> This is what I was missing .... basic quantum cell theory.
>
> I don't understand negative entropy ..... if entropy is randomness .......?
>
> I don't really quite understand Fractal either.......does anyone have something other than what wikipedia says?
> It seems to me that a lot of these terms are just something scientists latched onto for their NYT crossword puzzle.
>
> To me - Fractal sounds random too .... I thought I understood the meaning but when I looked it up ...I just go - holey cow!

Audrey, fractals are easy. Different phenomena showing remarkably similar patterns. Whirlpool, tornado, spinning galaxy, for example, are completely different phenomena but kind of look the same. They are exhibiting a fractal pattern. Look at a tree's leaf. It often resembles the tree--that is, it has veins that look like tree branches. The tree at is smallest element to its largest have the same pattern (not identical). Fractal!

### yewwei.tan — 2013-11-21T05:12:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-november-webinar.8803/post-101207>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=101180)
>
> I love, love, love this webinar! I haven't gotten all the way thru yet.
>
> This is what I was missing .... basic quantum cell theory.
>
> I don't understand negative entropy ..... if entropy is randomness .......?
>
> I don't really quite understand Fractal either.......does anyone have something other than what wikipedia says?
> It seems to me that a lot of these terms are just something scientists latched onto for their NYT crossword puzzle.
>
> To me - Fractal sounds random too .... I thought I understood the meaning but when I looked it up ...I just go - holey cow!

Regarding Negative Entropy -- entropy means "disorder" or "randomness", and negative entropy therefore means "more ordered" or "less random". Scientists like to use particular terminology , so don't let that trip you up!

Regarding fractals -- an example is the Sierpinski triangle [http://www.oftenpaper.net/sierpinski.htm](http://www.oftenpaper.net/sierpinski.htm) or the Koch Snowflake [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch\_snowflake](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

Nature uses fractals; a tree looks like its branches, and the branches on those branches look like more branches. Interestingly, our the capillaries that send blood to all your muscles look just like tree branches. Why? Perhaps it's because similar fractal patterns that a tree can use efficiently gather sunlight apply just as well to a blood vessel trying to send precious blood to hard-working muscles.

This allows nature to use very simple rules to create amazing detail; a tree branch "knows" how to grow itself using it's "parent's" pattern. It's purely a matter of efficiency! But we need a formal language for describing them of course, and hence the mathematical notion of fractal dimensions.

Sidenote: Nassim Nicholas Taleb loves to talk about fractals, how they apply to everyday life (giving credit to the guy who discovered them, Benoit Mandelbrot, who very recently passed away). Taleb's books are a great read, and his most recent 'Antifragile' is probably a long but approachable read.

### yewwei.tan — 2013-11-21T05:25:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-november-webinar.8803/post-101208>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=101139)
>
> What did CT 6 say? Cold sensitizes the hormone receptors to become more sensitive so therefore you dont need as much to live........I said in there you do not even need a thyroid gland in cold........because of this. The effect is called the Hall effect. And Becker found it.

How long does this hormone sensitising effect last after the cold stimulus is lost? Say you're CTing in summer time, and obviously you get the cold while in the water, but once you get out, you're in a warm environment. Even in winter time, would this also require that one be constantly exposed (peripheral receptors) to cold temperatures? Or is a case whereby a leptin sensitive person stays leptin sensitive until a stressor (lack of sleep, EMF, etc..) is made?

### caroline — 2013-11-21T08:44:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-november-webinar.8803/post-101228>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=101207)
>
> Regarding Negative Entropy -- entropy means "disorder" or "randomness", and negative entropy therefore means "more ordered" or "less random". Scientists like to use particular terminology , so don't let that trip you up!
>
> Regarding fractals -- an example is the Sierpinski triangle [http://www.oftenpaper.net/sierpinski.htm](http://www.oftenpaper.net/sierpinski.htm) or the Koch Snowflake [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch\_snowflake](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)
>
> Nature uses fractals; a tree looks like its branches, and the branches on those branches look like more branches. Interestingly, our the capillaries that send blood to all your muscles look just like tree branches. Why? Perhaps it's because similar fractal patterns that a tree can use efficiently gather sunlight apply just as well to a blood vessel trying to send precious blood to hard-working muscles.
>
> This allows nature to use very simple rules to create amazing detail; a tree branch "knows" how to grow itself using it's "parent's" pattern. It's purely a matter of efficiency! But we need a formal language for describing them of course, and hence the mathematical notion of fractal dimensions.
>
> Sidenote: Nassim Nicholas Taleb loves to talk about fractals, how they apply to everyday life (giving credit to the guy who discovered them, Benoit Mandelbrot, who very recently passed away). Taleb's books are a great read, and his most recent 'Antifragile' is probably a long but approachable read.

Thankyou so much for this ....

The tree at is smallest element to its largest have the same pattern (not identical). Fractal!

### prAna303 — 2013-11-21T10:51:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-november-webinar.8803/post-101232>

> I don't understand negative entropy ..... if entropy is randomness .......?

I like how Erwin SchrÃ¶dinger once wrote, not in exact words but...

When a unit (lifeform) do itÂ´s living, walking, talking, living a life in the environment that surround it that unit increase the entropy. When you increase entropy you quickly fast forward to a state of maximum entropy and that is death in different forms. The trip to that place can happend fast and when you reach some kind of level you slowly die more and more. You can be classed as dead as unit but the decay is slow from that point. Think how you felt when you did that crash when it was so warm, the you is 37C and the surroundings was 34C? Not much difference between life and death.

So life is about taking in negative forms of entropy.

When you take a whole day of living, think about what draws energy from you, not just the living you but also the factors from outside you. This is why training and eating less is a good way to kill your life. When you train, you can not think the ordinary way anymore. Motion is increasing the entropy in you and that you must compensate... Food stuff, real food can do that to a point but not as much as the nature, real nature can.

This is why i always tell my friends that training is only good when you have a balance in your energy. And that does not mean that you eat like the guru said in the facebook group you joined BUT a balance of energy that feeds you natural, not the energy that draws energy from the unit YOU. Thinking burning calories and training more is dead wrong, you draw all reservs of energy out of you in this case, crash and burn.

My way of getting negative entropy is being in the wild. When i do some time in places that draw energy from me i always compensate with nature. I get myself exposed with cold, wind, rain, snow, water, sun and magnetism every single day. I have done it for my whole life and it is from this moment in life i get the science behind it, thanks to this place.

By the way... Winter is here at last. Snow started to fall yesterday, -9C outside and i love it. Now i know why, the winter is negative entropy for a Swede, not going to Thailand in december like my friends do. People complain when the meet me in the mountains, i do my runs in shorts and thin shoes only... Yesterday i scared people when i once again sat against a tree, just looking at the snow, the thought i was dead... This is how off we have become, to see natural things is weird and wrong in todays world. This i fight to change slowly now by giving away bits and pieces of how i do my living.

### nonchalant — 2013-11-21T20:06:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-november-webinar.8803/post-101270>

I think negative entropy describes what I observed in late summer. All summer long I was happy that I could tolerate the heat so well. Similar to what I experienced last summer, just a bit better. Thanks CT! Then one day I was drenched in sweat. Dripping into my eyes. I was miserable. What was different? I was barefoot...

Then I realized it was a cloudy day. It was Texas hot, but not nearly as hot as the previous day, which was sunny. Without the sunshine, I didn't have the negative entropy, or at least not enough of it, to counteract all the entropy-producing heat!

I would have liked to explore this more, but darn it, the temperatures soon started to get mild. I still find it a bit difficult to believe that a hot sunny day is more tolerable than a hot cloudy day. I'll get to the bottom of this... in about half a year...

# Thread: In Light of the November Webinar, What about Alfred Pischinger?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-light-of-the-november-webinar-what-about-alfred-pischinger.8809/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2013-11-20T04:04:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-light-of-the-november-webinar-what-about-alfred-pischinger.8809/post-101081>

After listening to the November webinar, I am left wondering where Alfred Pischinger and the modern Humoral/Extracellular Matrix school fits in. They identified that the human organism is open thermodynamically and demonstrated quantam effects experimentally I believe. The recent reprint of his work by North Atlantic in 2007 covers most of his significant work. I do not pretend to be an authority and have resisted physics my whole life, yet I get it and I bought Feyneman's QED today. Strangely we had 5 books in our country house in Maine and one of them was "Surely You're joking". I read it every summer, but failed to catch the fire.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-20T05:44:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-light-of-the-november-webinar-what-about-alfred-pischinger.8809/post-101083>

Alfred Pischinger was an extension of Claude Bernard original theories. Bernard believed the "environment within", while referring to the extra-cellular fluid environment, and its physiological capacity to ensure protective stability for the tissues and organs of multicellular living organisms. These theories were all forebears of the idea of a morphogenic field without any concept of how it worked or was induced. Gilbert Ling did all the heavy lifting. And when I get to the Quantum Puzzle blog........you'll see how much he really did. You might be shocked to know that after Feynman won his Nobel he took a year off to focus on biology. He always knew QED scaled to biology but he never connect the dots. The reason was he did not read Frolich's work because Frolich left the partical world of the Standard Model and went to the emergent field of physics and began to use coherence and its scales. He was further ahead than any partical theorist. Feynman was a math guy......not a biology guy. The math of biology was not yet invented in Feyman's hey day.........Fractal geometry of Mandelbrot.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-20T05:52:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-light-of-the-november-webinar-what-about-alfred-pischinger.8809/post-101084>

Since Mandelbrot this field is now attracting many bright physicists........and my faith is in them and not biology or medicine to fix this mess. Today we now have links from QED built and developed and proved by Feynman to fractal geometry. There are deep connections between quantum and fractal dynamics that are just beginning to be explored. In 1996, Michael Berry at Bristol University in Britain discovered to his surprise that quantum systems exhibit fractal properties. Four years later in 2000, several physicists in Poland constructed fractal solutions of the SchrÃ¶dinger wave equation that are valid for a large class of quantum problems. This stunned me when I read it. It means everything is fractal, the cell, its reactions, , the vortex in the brain, and even time is fractal at its core. Even more fundamentally, French astrophysicist Laurent Nottale showed convincingly that the geometry of space-time becomes fractal when Einsteinâ€™s principle of relativity is extended to scale transformations. When you can make Einstein fractal it is part of the great design.

# Thread: Essential cofactors for PQQ?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/essential-cofactors-for-pqq.8810/>
**Subforum:** Mitochondrial Rx
**Posts:** 26

### Josh — 2013-11-20T04:19:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/essential-cofactors-for-pqq.8810/post-101082>

It seems as though PQQ requires other B vitamins as well as d Ribose and Ubiquinol to be present in adequate levels to function. What is the recommended synergy for its use?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-06T21:37:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/essential-cofactors-for-pqq.8810/post-102618>

Promise Iw ill get to this..............but I just do not have enough out on the blog about redox........Manganese is a big deal and yes it is a transition metal.

### Josh — 2013-12-07T19:58:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/essential-cofactors-for-pqq.8810/post-102740>

For what it is worth, I muscle tested this and found it to be relevant. Resonant testing using machines such as radionics or EAV based or with the body can be a perilous exercise as it is a challenge to create the proper challenge and then to interpret the data. The people I trust most verify all findings with either kinesiology or an EAV device that tests a single factor or set of factors in a circuit with the subject organism, human or other. The devices that automatically test many factors and circuits and produce reports are always biased by the experience of the people that build and program them. They can produce very useful data, but for me one must always understand the limitation of the programming bias. I use the body because it is more sensitive in my experience and does not need to be plugged in. This being said, I had tried the PQQ in combination with about 100 other single nutrients and formulas, but not manganese. At times it would improve a subject's resonance when combined with ubiquinol, B12 and occasionally d Ribose or Magnesium Glycinate. I am always surprised when it works out this way and many people have questioned such results as being too unreliable as they are subject to the bias of the tester. I have been surprised often enough to know that my bias does not mean much if I am testing properly. Being able to find evidence of effects such as these forces me to go back and try to learn the science to the point where I have a plausible physiological explanation for what I observe/test.

### Josh — 2013-12-08T01:25:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/essential-cofactors-for-pqq.8810/post-102762>

I took a look at the literature on Manganese and its relationship to PQQ. I am always amazed at the continuing assumption that trace mineral levels are adequate in the environment and food chain and that they are uniformly absorbed and used by people. If we remove those two assumptions which is a reasonable thing to do at this point in history, one would have some real issues without adequate Manganese and or the ability to assimilate and utilize it. Of course there is not much money or interest in studying a mineral that can not be made into a patented drug with high revenue potential so there is not much research. In the FDA we trust...transition element it will be subject to all of the EMF effects that would tend to leave an individual under stress either deficient in it and or unable to utilize what was in his or her system reliably. Without this, several important parts of human physiology are no longer assured to function well. Among other things it appears would be the loss of the enzyme function responsible for PQQ utilization? Curioser and curioser....I wonder if there is a study somewhere showing that while trace amounts are required for normal function, it requires high doses to restore a deficiency condition? I found one study that found this to be the case with Molybdenum. The recesses of my brain where redox chemistry may still live are beginning to take notice...

> Function:
> Manganese is a mineral element that is both nutritionally esse
> ntial and has the potential to be very toxic. This fact is
> further complicated by the small range of dosage for clinical
> benefit and toxicity with serious consequences. Manganese
> is an important factor in many critical biochemical proce
> sses including antioxidant functi
> on. The principle antioxidant
> enzyme within our mitochondria (energy) is superoxide
> dismutase and the enzymes requires manganese for optimal
> performance. Manganese is also required fo
> r normal skeletal development and cartilage synthesis. Wound healing is also
> impacted by manganese, as the synthesis
> of collagen in skin cells is dependent
> on the presence of adequate manganese.
> Manganese is also important functioning as a co-factor in th
> e metabolism of carbohydrates, amino acids and cholesterol.
> Manganese is considered anti-osteoporotic and anti-arthritic.
> Immunologic Effects of Symptoms:
> Reduced T-lymphocyte response to challenge
> and impaired ability to fight infection.
> Deficiency Symptoms:
> Deficiency in manganese is rare, but it is now estimated that
> up to 20% of the population may be deficient in manganese
> caused by improper diet and eating ha
> bits. Deficiency in manganese may lead to
> various health problems which could
> include bone malformation, eye and hearing problems, increa
> sed cholesterol, hypertension, infertility, cardiovascular
> issues, memory loss, hearing loss, muscle cramping and tremor
> s. Other deficiency symptoms may include ataxia, fainting
> and carbohydrate intolerance (diabetes). Manganese de
> ficiency has also been linked to myasthenia gravis.
> Manganese Toxicity: Symptoms of manganese toxicity
> mimic those of Parkinson’s disease with permanent
> neurological damage. It may also precipitate hypertension in
> patients over 40, and significant rises in manganese are
> found in patients with hepatitis, cirrhosi
> s and in dialysis patients and victims of
> heart attack. Early signs of toxicity inclu
> de
> loss of appetite, impaired memory, and mask-like facial e
> xpressions. Excess manganese w
> ill reduce iron absorption.

### Josh — 2013-12-08T05:25:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/essential-cofactors-for-pqq.8810/post-102791>

Thank you the reminder on Spectracell. The most interesting stuff that I have found is the recent research on SLC30A10 which among other things suggests how much is not known about the functions of Manganese. Digging through some other research, I found one interesting study that had a weak, but still interesting correlation between lower fat and or vegetarian diets and Manganese deficiency in spite of sufficient quantities in the diet suggesting lower bioavailability in some food sources. The study also showed that it took 90 days of supplementation to increase levels. I wonder if the trend towards higher iron levels in many people are created competitive inhibition, at least at a functional level with Manganese?

Without this, several important parts of human physiology are no longer assured to function well. Among other things it appears would be the loss of the enzyme function responsible for PQQ utilization? Curioser and curioser....I wonder if there is a study somewhere showing that while trace amounts are required for normal function, it requires high doses to restore a deficiency condition? I found one study that found this to be the case with Molybdenum. The recesses of my brain where redox chemistry may still live are beginning to take notice...

Isn't Manganese a known neurotoxin in excess? I seem to remember there is a really narrow range of benefit, which can be achieved by food, and the toxicity starts at a very low dose.

Seems like a very scary thing to be supplementing if you have unknown sources in the diet?

Compare that to magnesium, where it is relatively difficult to see a toxic dose from supplementation. Magnesium seems like a no brainer. Manganese seems to warrant a little more caution.

Is there a way to test manganese levels reliably, so you could see if your diet is supplying it?

### Josh — 2014-03-18T06:57:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/essential-cofactors-for-pqq.8810/post-116124>

Dude, you need to read the literature on methylation pathways and apply it to yourself, otherwise you are mucking around in the dark with regards to your own N=1. This is a huge factor in how "toxic/stressful" something will be in a given system. Almost no one gets what is understood to date and there is soooo much more to work out. Then you need to figure out what your inflammatory/oxidative stressors are. Then make your own plan or find someone to help you and do some work and make the experiment on yourself. For me, Manganese has been life changing over the last 6 months and I am not dead yet. Take to heart Dr. Kruse's insight regarding EMF having a disproportionate effect on transition metals which to me means that all bets on how much we need and whether they will work as planned/hoped is off. I will take my own risks having had a front row seat to medicine and dying doctors my whole life.

You might make a different evaluation such as what does life look like without enough manganese and what does life look like without enough magnesium. Apply it to your situation/N=1 and see what seems relevant. You might also look at the supplement companies selling doses as high as 40mg and figure they have done some risk assessment as they are seriously exposed. Dr. Yasko's work details the relevance and importance and I have seen recommendations from that world for 60mg/day. I take an average of 30mg/day. In that world they recommend alternating hair and urine testing. Good to start reading. I have read about 6000 pages since I got here.....

Best of luck as always.....

In that world they recommend alternating hair and urine testing. Good to start reading. I have read about 6000 pages since I got here.....
>
> Best of luck as always.....

How much manganese are you supplementing, and when/how do you take it?

I don't know enough about manganese to be critical of it in any specific dose. The general point was that \*some\* supplements have no toxicity at very large doses and real benefits at lower doses. So those supplements are a no-brainer, a real "free lunch".

Other supplements have definite toxicities at relatively low doses, and those supplements just require you to have knowledge and exercise care.

That's not a put-down of manganese. It's just about risk management in general.

### Josh — 2014-03-18T07:24:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/essential-cofactors-for-pqq.8810/post-116132>

As I suggested, it is worth spending some time with the people who have identified the issue led by Amy Yasko and her work on methylation. You can read what Dr. Kruse said about transition metals in the blogs. It really has a great deal to do with phenotypic expression with regards to methylation, your environment and the larger health issues one faces. In my perfunctory research, it seems that molybdenum and manganese are involved in a huge number of vital metalloenyme complexes and that there are many black boxes in our understanding to date. There is no money in minerals because they can not be patented. Of course this has not stopped the drug companies from making a prostate cancer drug out of molybdenum that probably has no more efficacy than a non-prescription amino acid chelate. Nor has it stopped the prescription of Lithium Carbonate as a prescrition drug while a less toxic and probably equally efficacious form is available that can be bought over the counter in Lithium Orotate. Look up the number of iatrogenic illnesses and deaths from manganese versus perhaps aspirin or penicillin....Have you checked the MMWR for your profession? Your living area?

[http://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/](http://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/)

> The Dose Makes the Poison:
>
> Here is a small sample of well-documented side effects, provided by the National Institutes of Health:
>
> • Swelling of the eyes, face, lips, tongue, or throat • Wheezing or difficulty breathing • Fast heartbeat • Fast breathing • Cold, clammy skin • Ringing in the ears • Loss of hearing • Bloody vomit • Bright red blood in stools
>
> This list should scare you. It should scare you because these aren’t side effects of anabolic steroids. These are common side effects of aspirin.
>
> Ferriss, Timothy (2010-12-14). The 4-Hour Body: An Uncommon Guide to Rapid Fat-Loss, Incredible Sex, and Becoming Superhuman (Kindle Locations 2939-2945). Random House, Inc.. Kindle Edition.

### kovita — 2014-03-18T08:10:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/essential-cofactors-for-pqq.8810/post-116139>

after ten days on minimal doses of lithium orotate, manganese and molybdenum I became extremaly sensitive to methyl groups. I am Double COMT homozygous and homozygous for VDR taq. My reaction corresponds to the literature, it seems like there is better transport of b12 to the cells. I cannot make any tests easily here to see what is happening as yasko does, but i know I need a lot of potassium now and had to lower methyl donors to minimal doses. CT starts up now methylation big time. Yesterday I got heavily overmethylated about 10 minutes after leaving the bath. I have been taking methyl donors for some time now and after being very sensitive on the beginning i went to much higher doses, now I am even on much lower doses than when i started initially without lithium, mangan and molybden.

# Thread: Dr. Jackson's boot amp .....week #2
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-jacksons-boot-amp-week-2.8811/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2013-11-20T06:06:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-jacksons-boot-amp-week-2.8811/post-101086>

Thanks Dr. Jackson and Dr. kruse and Misty and her team.

This boot camp is excellent - so much valuable information. I now can see so clearly what Dr. k. Always tells us.....it is all about context. I don't see how anyone could successfully navigate this on their own,

The potential for really screwing up is huge .....and doing untold damage.

### Josh — 2013-11-20T18:36:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-jacksons-boot-amp-week-2.8811/post-101121>

As I see it, Dr. Kruse managed exactly that. However, he did a lot of reading and experimenting and had access to testing and technology that may not be available to everyone. The hardest thing is to find someone as motivated as you are...to assist and guide. When you are in the most delicate group which implies epigenetic limitations with extreme sensitivity and issues with assimilating nutrients and absorbing environmental insults and variability it often takes people who have a similar experience to get in the trenches with you.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-20T21:17:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-jacksons-boot-amp-week-2.8811/post-101140>

What motivates you to change........Guesses. When you make guess they are called hypothesis. When you do experiments (testing your labs) on yourself you see if the work or not. Then you must realize you are the easiest person to fool yourself........so if your experiments do not fit the theory......your theory is wrong. What branch of science uses this pathway? PHYSICS.......which one does not? Biology. This is why we have statins.

Yep, the more I learn and read sometimes the more I get confused and don't know which way to go. I wish I could find a doctor around here that new about Dr. Kruse and the stuff on here so I could get some help, may have to do some digging again. I think I will be scheduling some time with Dr. Tim after I get my next set of labs drawn.

I had never heard about the iodine issue with the antibodies. I started the DD on them and she just recently had her thyroid tested and her TSH is high but I do not have the results from her anitbodies yet.

This was one I will have to listen to multiple times to get everything.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-20T21:51:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-jacksons-boot-amp-week-2.8811/post-101153>

Doctors dont learn physics.....they have to teach themselves.....to begin to help patients. This is why you need to learn it. You're motivated too and they are not.

### caroline — 2013-11-21T01:33:24+0200

This is what happened to me too....my TSH was crazy high and then my Doc sent me for a urine spot test and it was very low. Now he thinks I am hypothyroid ..... but I don't think I am - I have no symptoms .......well - I don't get QED - but ....
I don't really see how I could possibly be deficient when eating epi paleo and I was supplementing with iodine. I think I am just going to do a couple of drops a day.

My Doctor is so lovely but he just doesn't know enough about all this - so we are struggling together.

Right now I am just going by how I feel ......... I think, for me, it is a low PG issue and that is what JK told me long ago.....

### caroline — 2013-11-21T01:45:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-jacksons-boot-amp-week-2.8811/post-101178>

The hardest thing is to find someone as motivated as you are...to assist and guide. When you are in the most delicate group which implies epigenetic limitations with extreme sensitivity and issues with assimilating nutrients and absorbing environmental insults and variability it often takes people who have a similar experience to get in the trenches with you.

Dr. K. could make educated guesses about his own health and has so much at his disposal - not the least being his desire to fix himself.

I just was so struck by what Dr. Jackson said about supplements. We all tend to jump on the supplement bandwagon - hoping for a quick fix. You can spend incredible amounts of money on stuff and actually make things worse or, certainly, just be throwing money down the toilet. The supplement market must be a huge money making machine for a lot in Paleo and we are being sucked in with the latest, greatest thing to turn our titanic around.

Dr. K. has always said, probably from day #1, you can't out supplement a bad diet. we are always looking for the latest, greatest,easiest fix....

Mind you I did a stint of iodine supplementation with all the co's and had no probs........different story for DH...which brings me back to caution,caution, caution with supp's.

all very interesting isn't it.......

### caroline — 2013-11-21T07:37:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-jacksons-boot-amp-week-2.8811/post-101226>

I asked JK long ago about iodine supplementing (in a consult). And he said if you are eating epi paleo - I didn't need to.
N
Why didn't I listen? Really good question......

Question everything......

### Jude — 2013-11-21T23:55:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-jacksons-boot-amp-week-2.8811/post-101280>

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=101248)
>
> I have had NO issues with starting and upping the iodine plus I had a test that showed I was low on it. Now I am just so confused about what to do. I have dropped down to about 50 mg 1X a day from 2X a day.

I do understand your confusion ncild...think its generic here!.......so much information and good too....however the problem remains is that, is this information right for you and your N=1?

We have the unfortunate ability to inflict harm upon ourselves with this info if not used wisely and with proper support and there's the rub....where do you find same? JK advised us from the start of this blog that we all should have a professional guiding us during our journey to health.

I supplemented iodine to 100mgms for over 3mths with absolutely no seeming ill effects(with all co factors). Different story for DH....ended up with Atial fibrillation and hospitalisation..very scary....what we didn't know (and nor did the drs) was that he had multiple nodules on his thyroid which were stimulated by the iodine...hence causing the prob. Probably an extreme case but that scenario is possible with any supplementation...so hence repeating what JK says....use food as it provides us with safe levels of iodine.

In 2011 a few members ended up with Vit D levels of 350!:.........I was one....how could that happen with low D levels to start with?...what we didn't know was that taking Vit K2 with Vit D causes a synergistic effect ...resulting in high D levels over a couple of months......my GP was not overly concerned but laid down the law about daily amts....guess I'm now into caution in a big way.

>
> I supplemented iodine to 100mgms for over 3mths with absolutely no seeming ill effects(with all co factors). Different story for DH....ended up with Atial fibrillation and hospitalisation..very scary....what we didn't know (and nor did the drs) was that he had multiple nodules on his thyroid which were stimulated by the iodine...hence causing the prob. Probably an extreme case but that scenario is possible with any supplementation...so hence repeating what JK says....use food as it provides us with safe levels of iodine.
>
> In 2011 a few members ended up with Vit D levels of 350!:.........I was one....how could that happen with low D levels to start with?...what we didn't know was that taking Vit K2 with Vit D causes a synergistic effect ...resulting in high D levels over a couple of months......my GP was not overly concerned but laid down the law about daily amts....guess I'm now into caution in a big way.

But finding someone who know enough is so hard and Jack says that we need to ultimately educate ourselves. I wish there was a list someone of doctors that Jack knows that follows his ideas.

My doc did say that I should supplement with iodine but the levels he told me were so low. My iodine urine was 169 ug/l and Iodine/CR was 158 ug/g so pretty low when the ranges are 100-110 but how much I should take while eating more seafood and seaweed is beyond me. I guess that is something to add to my test list for next month.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Just got my AGE levels measured... holy mackerel!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-got-my-age-levels-measured-holy-mackerel.8815/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 26
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Inger — 2013-11-20T19:07:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-got-my-age-levels-measured-holy-mackerel.8815/post-101122>

My dad just had one of his friends for a visit.. they was about to go to a health event and his friend had gotten a AGE Reader (DiagnOptics Technologies BV, Groningen, The Netherlands) that works using skin autofluorescence (SAF), for the meeting. It is a pretty new thing, this link is about a study done

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3672176/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3672176/)

He said it costs about 3000 â‚¬ when I asked. He measured all our AGE levels and it was pretty interesting.... He is around 74 yo himself and looks very healthy, is a health guy so to speak. The AGE machine told his age to be 25. My mom was 54 (real age 61) and my dad had about the same numbers, a little worse. My bro had 23 (real age 21), he has not lived a healthy life until now, but since a month or so he does great, eats and does good stuff. I am proud of him. Now... I was pretty exited what my number will be... I just had labs done last week and my cholesterol was so high and my glucose too

HDL was 119 mg/dl
LDL was 193,3 mg/dl
Triglycerides 43,4 mg/dl

glucose was 108 mg/dl

and I am eating so much fat and I though holey moley I will have so much AEG in my body....... but.. my biologic age according to the machine was 5...???! I had the best by far! They were all stunned.. but I figure it is because I eat almost 100% raw for almost 10 years? When I do not cook anything (almost) there will be way less AGEs? Or is it the lifestyle I am doing? The Kruse style? IDK but I am so happy now! You see... I do have been warned from some health (raw food) people that eating so much protein and fat and coconut oil will make huge amounts of AGEs in my body and it will be very bad for my health and I will age fast. I did not listen to them and now it seems my diet is just right. Maybe my high glucose levels are not bad at all either, maybe my body have just made some anti freeze to take me safe through the winter

I was not too surprised though to be honest - I really feel like I am 5 yo

That's awesome Inger... I bet is a combination of everything your doing, as well as where you living...

Inger your high glucose levels are due to the CT......below 62 F insulin wont work in humans......so this is why BG goes up......and it is a natural phenomena in the part of the world you live in.......but when you warm up like a modern humans and have the high BG from carbs......you get Diabetes which is why the Norwegian countries have the highest rates of T1 D in the world. BG matters little in a cold environment.....it is actually an epigenetic adaptation to cold. See The evolutionary friend or foe blog for the details

# Thread: Butters' Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/butters-journal.8818/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 951
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Butters — 2013-11-20T21:24:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/butters-journal.8818/post-101146>

Hello,

this is a optimal journal start from me, a student of sports science from germany. I will do my best to live an optimal life and solve the problems appearing in a student's everyday life.
The entries will be very short, I will do my best to post only the important things.

Here are aims I am shooting for the next weeks/month:

\-improve sleep
\-achieving optimal body composition
\-lay optimal hormonal foundation for muscle building
\-improve resistance against the cold
\-prevent illness

I will do CT most days of the week using the protocoll and try to eat within the light cycle only (dinner may differ 1-2 hours).
I will try to eat good quality food, but often it isn't possible because of the limitted student's budget  but I will do my best.
I have to choose a weight-training routine, since I think I have a good leptin sensitivity, to build more muscle/strength. This must function with a ketogenic diet. I've been reading many articles and think to use HIT principles.

Until now I have skipped breakfast and have used a IF-regime. I'm not hungry at all in the morning hours. We'll see how I'll change variables.
For CT I'm using face-dunks in tap water sometimes with cubes. In addition I use cold showers and try to improve my time at the coldest temperature (I turn it colder while I'm standing under it). After just one week the body starts heating quickly.

First Question:

Is it better for the hormones to skip dinner if I can't eat until 7pm and eat the next meal not until the next morning?

### Inger — 2013-11-20T21:57:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/butters-journal.8818/post-101155>

Welcome to journal land Alex!

I think it is better for your hormones to skip dinner if so late... and eat a breakfast instead. Because your sleep will be better, you should not eat 4 hours before going to bed. Good sleep = better hormones
But IDK... context.. if you are healthy and young and have no issues maybe it is not worst thing to do to eat shortly after 7 PM?

### Butters — 2013-11-20T22:14:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/butters-journal.8818/post-101159>

Would have been 8.30-9.00. It doesn't bother me to fast either, because like I wrote I now can get away with skipping meals without getting hungry. So if there is a hormonal advantage (can be sleep) to skip the meal if it's so late I would to it.

### smart cat — 2013-11-20T23:37:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/butters-journal.8818/post-101161>

There is a hormonal advantage: Growth Hormone, autophagy. Crappy house cleaning at night >> accelerated aging.

### caroline — 2013-11-21T01:14:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/butters-journal.8818/post-101171>

Sleep is the most important thing as smart cat says.
